# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of coincidence detection in PET imaging. Why is this called "electronic collimation"?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

Coincidence detection is the simultaneous registration of two **511 keV** annihilation photons by a pair of opposing detectors within a narrow time window (**6 – 12 ns**). The straight line connecting the two firing detectors is called the **Line of Response (LOR)** and is assumed to pass through the annihilation point.

This is called **electronic collimation** because the timing coincidence restricts acceptance to only those photons that originated from the *same* annihilation event. Unlike SPECT (which uses a physical lead collimator to restrict photon trajectories), PET uses *time-correlation* of detector pairs to achieve the same geometric constraint — hence **electronic** instead of mechanical. This improves sensitivity dramatically because no photons are absorbed by a physical collimator. **(3 Marks — full credit requires mentioning "511 keV", "time window", "Line of Response", and contrast with physical collimator)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain Abbe's resolution limit for an optical microscope. What are the two ways to improve resolution?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Statement:**

$$
d = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{NA}
$$

where **d** is the smallest resolvable distance, **λ** is the wavelength of illumination, and **NA** is the numerical aperture (n · sin θ) of the objective.

**Explanation:** Because light diffracts through the finite aperture of the objective, each point in the specimen is imaged as an Airy disk rather than a perfect point. Two points closer than **d** cannot be distinguished.

**Two ways to improve resolution:**
1. **Reduce λ** — use shorter wavelengths (UV microscopy, X-ray microscopy, electron microscopy where λ becomes sub-nanometer).
2. **Increase NA** — use higher refractive index immersion media (oil NA ≈ 1.4, water NA ≈ 1.33) and wider collection cone half-angles. **(3 Marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question B — Option A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Explain the physics of positron emission and annihilation in PET. Derive the expression for the energy of annihilation photons. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various factors affecting the spatial resolution of a PET scanner with supporting mathematical expressions. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Beta-plus decay:** A proton-rich radionuclide (e.g., **¹⁸F**) decays by converting an internal proton into a neutron, emitting a positron (e⁺) and a neutrino:

$$
p \rightarrow n + e^{+} + \nu_e
$$

The **¹⁸F** parent nuclide becomes stable **¹⁸O**.

**Step 2 — Positron thermalization:** The emitted positron travels a short distance (the **positron range**, typically 0.5 – 2.5 mm for ¹⁸F, larger for higher-energy emitters like ¹⁵O) losing kinetic energy through Coulomb scattering with atomic electrons in tissue.

**Step 3 — Annihilation:** Once the positron is nearly at rest, it interacts with a nearby electron. Because they are antiparticles, they annihilate, converting their combined rest mass into electromagnetic radiation. To conserve both energy and (zero) momentum, **exactly two photons** are produced, emitted in **opposite directions**.

**Step 4 — Energy derivation:**

$$
\text{Total rest energy} = 2 m_0 c^{2}
$$

By conservation of momentum and energy:

$$
E_{\gamma 1} = E_{\gamma 2} = m_0 c^{2} = \mathbf{511\,keV}
$$

The pair sum is **1022 keV**. **(7 Marks complete)**

---

#### (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

The total spatial resolution of a PET system is degraded by three statistically independent factors:

$$
R_{sys} = \sqrt{R_{det}^{2} + R_{pos}^{2} + R_{nc}^{2}}
$$

**Factor 1 — Detector intrinsic resolution (R_det):**
Finite crystal dimensions cause positional uncertainty. For a crystal of width **w**:

$$
R_{det} \approx \frac{w}{2}
$$

Modern LYSO crystals achieve ~4 mm resolution; pixelated detectors with **~2 mm** crystals reduce this.

**Factor 2 — Positron range (R_pos):**
Before annihilation, the positron travels a short distance. Higher β⁺ endpoint energy → longer range → worse resolution.

$$
R_{pos} \propto \sqrt{E_{max}}
$$

Examples: ¹⁸F (E_max = 0.633 MeV) → ~0.5 mm; ¹⁵O (E_max = 1.732 MeV) → ~1.5 mm.

**Factor 3 — Non-collinearity (R_nc):**
Small residual momentum at annihilation causes deviation from 180° (Δθ ≈ 0.25° FWHM):

$$
R_{nc} = \frac{0.0022 \cdot D}{2}
$$

where **D** is the scanner ring diameter. Larger rings → larger blur. Whole-body PET (D = 80 cm) → R_nc ≈ 0.88 mm.

**Numerical example:** For a typical whole-body scanner with **R_det = 4 mm**, **R_pos = 0.5 mm** (¹⁸F), and **D = 80 cm**:

$$
R_{sys} = \sqrt{4^{2} + 0.5^{2} + 0.88^{2}} = \sqrt{17.02} \approx 4.13\,\text{mm}
$$

**Mitigation:** TOF-PET, depth-of-interaction (DOI) detectors, smaller crystals, and shorter-ring brain PET scanners. **(7 Marks complete)**

---

### Question B — Option B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM). Derive the relativistic de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through 100 kV. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare optical microscopy, confocal microscopy, and electron microscopy in terms of resolution, source, contrast mechanism, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Working Principle of TEM (Block Diagram Description):**

A TEM uses a beam of high-energy electrons (60 – 300 kV) generated by an **electron gun** (thermionic or field-emission). The electron beam is focused by **electromagnetic condenser lenses** onto an ultra-thin specimen (~50 – 100 nm thick). Electrons transmitted through the specimen are focused by **objective, intermediate, and projector lenses** to form a magnified image on a fluorescent screen or CCD camera.

**Steps in TEM operation:**
1. **Electron emission** — tungsten filament or LaB₆ crystal heated to ~2700 K emits electrons via thermionic emission (or field emission for higher brightness).
2. **Acceleration** — electrons accelerated through **60 – 300 kV** potential difference in a high-vacuum column (~10⁻⁶ Pa).
3. **Condenser lens system** — demagnifies the source and illuminates the specimen with a parallel or convergent beam.
4. **Specimen interaction** — electrons scatter elastically (give diffraction contrast) and inelastically (give compositional/Z-contrast). Only ultra-thin specimens allow transmission.
5. **Objective lens** — forms the first magnified image; an aperture (objective aperture) selects scattered vs unscattered electrons for **bright-field** or **dark-field** imaging.
6. **Intermediate and projector lenses** — further magnify (total magnification up to **10⁶×**).
7. **Image recording** — fluorescent screen + CCD camera.

**Relativistic de Broglie Wavelength Derivation (at V = 100 kV):**

From relativistic energy-momentum relation:

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_0 c^{2}}\right)}}
$$

Substituting values:
- h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s
- m₀ = 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg
- e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- V = 10⁵ V
- c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s

Relativistic correction:

$$
\frac{eV}{2 m_0 c^{2}} = \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-14}}{1.640 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 0.0977
$$

Final wavelength:

$$
\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{3.204 \times 10^{-44}}} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.790 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 3.70 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{m} = \mathbf{0.0037\,nm}
$$

**(7 Marks complete)**

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Optical Microscopy | Confocal Microscopy | Electron Microscopy (TEM/SEM) |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| **Source** | Visible light (λ ≈ 400–700 nm) | Laser (λ ≈ 400–700 nm) | Electron beam (λ ≈ 0.0037 nm @ 100 kV) |
| **Resolution** | ~200 – 500 nm (Abbe limit) | ~180 – 250 nm (lateral); ~500 nm (axial) | TEM: 0.05 – 0.2 nm; SEM: 1 – 10 nm |
| **Contrast mechanism** | Absorption, staining, fluorescence | Fluorescence + pinhole rejection of out-of-focus light | Scattering (elastic/inelastic), diffraction, Z-contrast |
| **Specimen preparation** | Thin sections, staining possible | Fluorescent labeling required | TEM: ultra-thin sections (<100 nm); SEM: conductive coating (gold) |
| **Depth of field** | Shallow | Optical sectioning (~0.5 µm) | TEM: 2D projection; SEM: large DoF giving 3D-like surface view |
| **Magnification range** | 4× – 1500× | 100× – 10,000× | TEM: 100× – 10⁶×; SEM: 10× – 500,000× |
| **Environment** | Ambient air, hydrated samples | Ambient or live-cell imaging | High vacuum required (cryo-EM exception) |
| **Sample type** | Live or fixed cells, tissues | Fluorescently labeled live/fixed cells | Fixed, dehydrated, sectioned (or cryo) specimens |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Very high |
| **Applications** | Histopathology, hematology, microbiology | 3D cellular imaging, subcellular localization | Virus imaging, crystallography, nanomaterials, organelle ultrastructure |

**Key advantages:** Optical = cheapest, live-cell friendly. Confocal = 3D optical sectioning, eliminates out-of-focus blur. Electron = sub-nanometer resolution, atomic-scale imaging.

**(7 Marks complete)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up which constant** goes with Abbe's equation: students often write 0.5 (coherent) instead of 0.61 (incoherent) — for *microscopes* with broad-band illumination, the correct factor is **0.61**.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in non-collinearity formula: the contribution is **(0.0022 × D) / 2**, not **0.0022 × D**.
> 3. **Confusing PET resolution factors** in quadrature: students sometimes *add* instead of *root-sum-square*. Always remember: independent Gaussian blurs combine in **quadrature**.
> 4. **Using non-relativistic de Broglie formula** for TEM: at 100 kV, the relativistic correction is **~10%** and *cannot be ignored*. Always use the full relativistic expression.
> 5. **Omitting units** in numerical answers (mm vs cm, eV vs keV vs MeV). KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
> 6. **Wrong "Line of Response" description**: students sometimes say the LOR is where the annihilation occurred — it is the *line connecting detectors*, with the annihilation assumed to be *somewhere on it*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### PET — Key Takeaways
- **511 keV** per annihilation photon; **1022 keV** total pair energy from **e⁺ + e⁻** annihilation.
- **¹⁸F-FDG** is the workhorse radiotracer (T₁/₂ = **109.8 min**).
- PET uses **electronic collimation** via coincidence detection (no physical collimator).
- **Coincidence window** ≈ **6 – 12 ns**.
- **Total spatial resolution (R_sys)** is the quadrature sum of **detector resolution**, **positron range**, and **non-collinearity**.
- **Non-collinearity contribution** = **(0.0022 × D) / 2** where D is scanner diameter.
- **TOF-PET** localizes along LOR by **Δx = (c · Δt) / 2**, improving SNR by √(D/Δx).
- PET reveals **function/metabolism**; CT/MRI reveals **anatomy** — hybrid PET/CT and PET/MRI combine both.
- **Event types**: true, scatter, random — only true events carry diagnostic information.

### Microscopy — Key Takeaways
- **Abbe diffraction limit**: **d = 0.61 λ / NA** for incoherent (optical microscope) imaging.
- **NA = n · sin θ** — increased by immersion oil (n = 1.515).
- **Optical resolution cap** ≈ **200 nm** with best optical microscopes.
- **Total magnification** = M_obj × M_eye (product, not sum).
- **Useful magnification limit** ≈ **1000 × NA / λ** — beyond this is "empty magnification."
- **Depth of field** improves at high NA and low magnification.
- **Confocal microscopy** uses a pinhole to reject out-of-focus light → 3D optical sectioning.
- **Fluorescence microscopy** uses fluorophores (GFP, DAPI, etc.) for specific molecular labeling.
- **Electron microscopy** breaks the diffraction limit by using **electrons** (λ ≈ 0.0037 nm at 100 kV) instead of photons.
- **TEM** transmits electrons through ultra-thin specimens → 2D internal image.
- **SEM** scans surface with focused electron beam → 3D-like topographical image with large depth of field.
- **Relativistic correction** for electron wavelength **cannot be ignored** above ~50 kV acceleration.

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- Both PET and microscopy are **non-invasive** (or minimally invasive) biomedical imaging techniques.
- Both follow the **resolution-vs-signal trade-off**: higher resolution requires more signal/sophistication.
- Both produce images that require **image processing algorithms** for quantitative interpretation.
- Hybrid systems (PET/CT, PET/MRI, correlative light-and-electron microscopy — CLEM) bridge functional and structural imaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** For PET, *always remember the number 511*. For microscopy, *always remember the number 0.61*. These two constants appear in nearly every PET/microscopy problem and forgetting them costs easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Acquisition and Reconstruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET636 - BIOMEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Image Acquisition and Reconstruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

**Image Acquisition** in biomedical engineering refers to the systematic, mathematically governed process of capturing physical or physiological signals emitted from, transmitted through, or reflected by biological tissue and converting them into a discrete digital representation suitable for storage, processing, and display. This process is governed by the physics of the chosen imaging modality (X-ray attenuation, magnetic resonance, ultrasonic echo, gamma emission) and is mathematically bounded by the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** and the **Modulation Transfer Function (MTF)** of the imaging chain.

**Image Reconstruction** is the inverse problem — the computational algorithm that transforms the acquired projection data (often in the form of sinograms, k-space trajectories, or raw sensor readings) into a spatially meaningful 2D cross-sectional image or 3D volumetric dataset. The most rigorous mathematical framework for tomographic reconstruction is the **Radon Transform** and its inverse, principally realized through the **Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem)** and the **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** This topic forms the foundational backbone for understanding CT, MRI, PET, and Ultrasound. Examiners frequently test the relationship between sampling theory, Radon space, and image domain.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Shadow Puppet" Reconstruction

Imagine you are blindfolded and placed in a room with an unknown 3D object. You are allowed to shine a flashlight from any direction and trace the shadow (projection) cast on a flat screen.

- **Acquisition** = Shining the light from many angles and recording each shadow profile (a *projection*).
- **Reconstruction** = Mathematically combining all these 1D shadow profiles to deduce the original 2D cross-section of the object.

If you take projections from only one angle, you cannot determine the object's shape (insufficient data). Take projections from **180°** of angles (CT) or fill the **k-space trajectory** (MRI), and you can uniquely reconstruct the object — analogous to solving a system of linear equations where each angle provides a new independent constraint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Frequency Rule of Thumb (Nyquist):** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, where $f_s$ is the sampling rate and $f_{max}$ is the highest spatial frequency in the object. For a clinical CT detector of pixel pitch $\Delta x$, the maximum resolvable spatial frequency is $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \Delta x}$ line-pairs per mm. Sampling below this limit produces **aliasing artifacts** (streaks and moiré patterns).

### Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Spatial Resolution:** typically **0.5 mm to 1.0 mm** for clinical CT, **0.5 mm to 2.0 mm** for MRI, **0.3 mm to 1.0 mm** for Ultrasound at clinical depths.
- **Dynamic Range:** **12-bit (4096 levels)** for CT, **16-bit (65536 levels)** for MRI.
- **Frame Rate (Real-time):** **30–60 fps** for fluoroscopy; **1000+ fps** for high-speed ultrasound.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Image Formation Chain (End-to-End Logic)

The complete biomedical image acquisition-reconstruction pipeline follows a strict signal-processing cascade:

1. **Source Generation:** Photons (X-ray), RF pulses (MRI), acoustic waves (US), or gamma rays (Nuclear) interact with tissue.
2. **Tissue Interaction:** Attenuation, relaxation, reflection, or emission carries diagnostic information proportional to physiological properties.
3. **Detection:** Sensors (scintillator + photodiode, RF coil, piezoelectric transducer, gamma camera) convert energy into analog electrical signals.
4. **Sampling & Quantization:** ADC samples the analog signal at rate $f_s$ and assigns discrete amplitude levels (quantization depth $b$ bits).
5. **Raw Data Storage:** Data is stored in a domain-specific format — *sinogram* (CT), *k-space* (MRI), *RF envelope* (US).
6. **Reconstruction Algorithm:** Mathematical inversion transforms raw data into a spatial image (FBP, FFT, Iterative methods).
7. **Post-Processing & Display:** Windowing, filtering, and mapping to a grayscale or color map for clinical interpretation.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why sample at all?** Continuous analog signals cannot be stored or processed by digital computers without discretization.
- **How does reconstruction "undo" acquisition?** Acquisition is a forward operator $\mathcal{A}$ mapping the object $f(x,y)$ to measurements $g$. Reconstruction computes the inverse $\mathcal{A}^{-1}$, which is **ill-posed** in many modalities and requires regularization (e.g., ramp filter in CT).
- **Why filter in FBP?** Simple back-projection smears projections back across the image, blurring the result with a $\frac{1}{r}$ point-spread function. The **ramp filter** (multiplication by $\vert \omega \vert$ in frequency domain) deconvolves this blur, recovering sharp edges.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Criterion | Hz |
| 2 | $N_q = 2^{b}$ | Quantization levels (dynamic range) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R(\theta, s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta,\; s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta)\,dt$ | Radon Transform (CT Projection) | line integrals |
| 4 | $\mathcal{F}\{R(\theta, s)\} = F(\omega\cos\theta,\; \omega\sin\theta)$ | Central Slice Theorem | Fourier pair |
| 5 | $b(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} R(\theta,\, x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)\,d\theta$ | Simple Back-Projection (SBP) | image domain |
| 6 | $f(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(\omega,\theta) \vert \omega \vert e^{j2\pi\omega s} d\omega \right] d\theta$ | Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) | reconstructed image |
| 7 | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\mu_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | decibels (dB) |
| 8 | $\text{MTF}(\nu) = \dfrac{\vert F_{image}(\nu) \vert}{\vert F_{object}(\nu) \vert}$ | Modulation Transfer Function | line-pairs/mm |
| 9 | $k_x = \dfrac{\gamma G_x t}{2\pi},\quad k_y = \dfrac{\gamma G_y t}{2\pi}$ | MRI k-space trajectory | radians/m |
| 10 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{2 d \cos\theta}{c}$ | Ultrasound Time-of-Flight | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** The Central Slice Theorem is the *Rosetta Stone* of tomographic reconstruction. It states that the 1D Fourier Transform of a projection at angle $\theta$ is exactly one radial line through the 2D Fourier Transform of the object. This bridges the projection domain and the image domain.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radiation Therapy Planning:** FBP-reconstructed CT images provide the electron density maps required for dose calculations in linear accelerators (LINAC).
- **Image-Guided Surgery:** Real-time 3D ultrasound reconstruction using voxel-by-voxel back-projection enables intraoperative navigation.
- **AI in Imaging:** Modern deep learning reconstruction (e.g., NVIDIA Clara, GE TrueFidelity) uses neural networks as learned regularizers, replacing the classical ramp filter with a data-driven inverse.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are exhaustive and aligned with the standard KTU board-level solution style — every algebraic step is shown, with a text row explaining the underlying logic.

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem (Reconstruction Constraint)

**Goal:** Prove that a band-limited continuous signal $x_c(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples $x_s(nT)$ provided $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Define the Ideal Sampling Operation**

Sampling is mathematically modeled as multiplication by a Dirac comb (impulse train) with period $T$:

$$
x_s(t) = x_c(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT)
$$

**Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform**

Using the modulation property of the Dirac comb in the frequency domain:

$$
X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c(f - k f_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Spectrum**

The sampled spectrum is a periodic replication of the original spectrum $X_c(f)$, centered at multiples of $f_s$. Each replica is scaled by $\frac{1}{T}$.

**Step 4 — Apply the No-Aliasing Condition**

To prevent spectral overlap (aliasing), adjacent replicas must not overlap. The original spectrum extends from $-f_{max}$ to $+f_{max}$ (total width $2 f_{max}$). The replicas are spaced by $f_s$. Non-overlap requires:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

**Step 5 — Reconstruction via Low-Pass Filtering**

If the no-aliasing condition holds, $X_c(f)$ can be recovered by passing $X_s(f)$ through an ideal low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = f_{max}$ and gain $T$. The inverse Fourier Transform gives the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula:**

$$
x_c(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_s(nT) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\dfrac{t - nT}{T}\right)
$$

**Conclusion:** Perfect reconstruction is achievable **if and only if** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Radon Transform

**Goal:** Express the line integral of an object $f(x,y)$ along an arbitrary line parameterized by angle $\theta$ and signed distance $s$ from the origin.

**Step 1 — Define the Line Equation**

A straight line in 2D can be written in Hessian normal form:

$$
s = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the Dirac Delta**

To restrict the 2D integration to this line only, embed the line equation inside a Dirac delta:

$$
R(\theta, s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \; \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - s) \; dx \, dy
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Inner Integral**

Choose rotated coordinates aligned with the line. Let $s = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ (along the line) and $t = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$ (perpendicular). The Jacobian of this rotation is unity. The delta function forces $s$ to be constant, leaving only integration over $t$:

$$
R(\theta, s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta,\; s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta) \; dt
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result**

$R(\theta, s)$ is the total "mass" of $f(x,y)$ along the line at distance $s$ from the origin, oriented at angle $\theta$. In CT, this is the **attenuation integral** measured by the detector.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Central Slice (Fourier Slice) Theorem

**Goal:** Show that the 1D Fourier Transform of the projection at angle $\theta$ equals a radial slice through the 2D Fourier Transform of the object.

**Step 1 — Start with the 1D FT of the Projection**

$$
S(\omega, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R(\theta, s) \; e^{-j2\pi\omega s} \, ds
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Radon Transform Definition**

$$
S(\omega, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta,\; s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta) \, dt \right] e^{-j2\pi\omega s} \, ds
$$

**Step 3 — Reverse the Order of Integration**

$$
S(\omega, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \; e^{-j2\pi(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)\omega} \, dx \, dy
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Standard 2D Fourier Transform**

Let $u = \omega\cos\theta$ and $v = \omega\sin\theta$. Then the integral becomes:

$$
S(\omega, \theta) = F(u, v) = F(\omega\cos\theta,\; \omega\sin\theta)
$$

**Conclusion:** The 1D FT of a projection at angle $\theta$ is a radial line through the 2D FT of the object, oriented at the same angle $\theta$ in frequency space. This is the **Fourier Slice Theorem**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)

**Goal:** Derive the analytical inversion formula for the Radon Transform.

**Step 1 — Start from the 2D Inverse Fourier Transform**

$$
f(x, y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(u, v) \; e^{j2\pi(ux + vy)} \, du \, dv
$$

**Step 2 — Switch to Polar Coordinates in Frequency Domain**

Let $u = \omega\cos\theta$ and $v = \omega\sin\theta$. The Jacobian for the transformation is $\vert \omega \vert$:

$$
f(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta) \; \vert \omega \vert \; e^{j2\pi\omega(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega \, d\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Central Slice Theorem**

By the Fourier Slice Theorem, $F(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta) = S(\omega, \theta)$:

$$
f(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(\omega, \theta) \; \vert \omega \vert \; e^{j2\pi\omega s} \, d\omega \right] d\theta
$$

where $s = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$.

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result**

- The **inner integral** is the inverse 1D FT of $S(\omega, \theta)$ multiplied by the **ramp filter** $\vert \omega \vert$. This produces the *filtered projection* at angle $\theta$.
- The **outer integral** smears (back-projects) this filtered projection across the image plane at angle $\theta$.

**Final FBP Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\, f(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(\omega, \theta) \; \vert \omega \vert \; e^{j2\pi\omega (x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega \right] d\theta \,}
$$

The factor $\vert \omega \vert$ is the **ramp filter** in the frequency domain; in practice, it is implemented as a convolution with the corresponding kernel (e.g., Shepp-Logan, Hamming-windowed ramp) in the spatial domain to avoid the singularities at high frequencies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CT Image Acquisition and Reconstruction Pipeline

The diagram below maps the complete signal-processing chain from X-ray source emission through to the displayed reconstructed image. It is designed as a *functional architecture flow* rather than a physical layout, suitable for KTU examination purposes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[X-RAY TUBE] --> A2[COLLIMATOR]
    A2 --> A3[PATIENT PHANTOM]
    A3 --> A4[DETECTOR ARRAY]
    A4 --> A5[LOG AMPLIFIER]
    A5 --> A6[ADC 12-BIT]
    A6 --> A7[SINOGRAM MEMORY]
    A7 --> A8[REBINNING]
    A8 --> A9[RAMP FILTER H]
    A9 --> A10[BACK PROJECTOR]
    A10 --> A11[IMAGE MATRIX 512X512]
    A11 --> A12[WINDOW LEVEL]
    A12 --> A13[DISPLAY MONITOR]
    
    A14[GANTRY CONTROLLER] --> A1
    A14 --> A4
    A14 --> A8
    A14 --> A10
    
    A15[RECONSTRUCTION CPU] --> A7
    A15 --> A9
    A15 --> A10
    A15 --> A11
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Top row: signal path from photon generation to image display.
- Bottom row: control and computational subsystems orchestrating acquisition and reconstruction in real time.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Fourier Slice Theorem Interpretation

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[OBJECT DOMAIN f OF X Y] --> B2[ACQUIRE PROJECTION AT ANGLE THETA]
    B2 --> B3[1D FOURIER TRANSFORM]
    B3 --> B4[RADIAL SLICE IN 2D FREQUENCY DOMAIN]
    B4 --> B5[COLLECT SLICES FROM 0 TO PI]
    B5 --> B6[2D INVERSE FOURIER TRANSFORM]
    B6 --> B7[RECONSTRUCTED IMAGE f OF X Y]
    
    B8[RAMP FILTER OMEGA] --> B6
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each projection angle $\theta$ produces one radial line in the 2D Fourier plane. Acquiring projections over $[0, \pi)$ fills the 2D frequency plane; a 2D inverse FFT (or the equivalent FBP integral) yields the reconstructed spatial image.

---

### 4.3 Reconstruction Algorithm Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1[START] --> C2{IS DATA COMPLETE?}
    C2 --> C3[YES] --> C4[USE FBP]
    C2 --> C5[NO LIMITED ANGLE] --> C6[USE ITERATIVE ART]
    C6 --> C7[CONVERGENCE CHECK]
    C7 --> C8{ERROR BELOW THRESHOLD}
    C8 --> C9[YES] --> C10[OUTPUT IMAGE]
    C8 --> C11[NO] --> C12[REPEAT ITERATION]
    C12 --> C7
    C4 --> C10
```

**Reading the diagram:** When the sinogram is complete and noise is moderate, **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** is the fastest and preferred algorithm. In limited-angle or high-noise scenarios (e.g., dental CBCT, low-dose CT), **iterative reconstruction (ART, SIRT, MLEM)** is used for its superior noise-handling and robustness to missing data.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> **Define the Radon Transform. Explain its physical significance in X-ray Computed Tomography.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Radon Transform** of a 2D function $f(x,y)$ is defined as the line integral of the function along a straight line parameterized by angle $\theta$ and signed distance $s$ from the origin:

$$
R(\theta, s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta,\; s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta) \, dt
$$

**Physical Significance:** In X-ray CT, the Radon Transform directly models the measurement made by a detector array. Each detector element records the total attenuation of the X-ray beam along its straight-line path through the patient. The collection of all such line integrals over different angles $\theta \in [0, \pi)$ forms the **sinogram**, which is the raw input to the reconstruction algorithm. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for physical interpretation)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**  
> **State and explain the Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem) with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Central Slice Theorem** states that the 1D Fourier Transform of a parallel-beam projection of an object $f(x,y)$ acquired at angle $\theta$ is equal to a radial line (slice) through the 2D Fourier Transform of the object, taken at the same angle $\theta$:

$$
\mathcal{F}_1\{R(\theta, s)\} = F(\omega\cos\theta,\; \omega\sin\theta)
$$

**Significance:** The theorem establishes a direct correspondence between projections in the spatial domain and radial lines in the frequency domain. By acquiring projections over $[0, \pi)$ and filling the 2D frequency plane, the object $f(x,y)$ can be reconstructed using the 2D inverse Fourier Transform. This is the theoretical foundation of **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**. **(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for equation, 1 for application)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Paper, Modified for Module 1 Coverage]*

### (a) Derive the Radon Transform for a 2D object $f(x,y)$ and discuss its key mathematical properties. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Line Parameterization:** A line in 2D can be expressed in Hessian normal form as $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = s$, where $s$ is the perpendicular distance from the origin and $\theta$ is the angle the perpendicular makes with the x-axis.

**Step 2 — Embedding with Dirac Delta:** To restrict a 2D integral to this single line, introduce a Dirac delta:

$$
R(\theta, s) = \iint f(x,y) \, \delta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - s) \, dx \, dy
$$

**Step 3 — Rotated Coordinates:** Define $s = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ and $t = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$. The Jacobian is 1. After evaluation:

$$
R(\theta, s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta,\; s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta) \, dt
$$

**Key Properties (4 marks for discussion):**
1. **Linearity:** $R\{a f_1 + b f_2\} = a R\{f_1\} + b R\{f_2\}$
2. **Periodicity in $\theta$:** $R(\theta + \pi, s) = R(\theta, -s)$ — only $[0, \pi)$ is unique.
3. **Rotation Invariance:** Rotating the object by $\phi$ rotates its Radon transform by $\phi$.
4. **Fourier Slice Property:** The 1D FT of $R(\theta, s)$ gives a radial line in 2D frequency space.

**(Marks distribution: 3 for derivation, 4 for properties and discussion)**

---

### (b) Explain the Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) algorithm with the ramp filter derivation. Discuss its advantages over simple back-projection. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Limitation of Simple Back-Projection:** Direct back-projection $b(x,y) = \int_0^\pi R(\theta, x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)\, d\theta$ smears each projection back across the image, creating a blurred result with a $\frac{1}{r}$ point-spread function (PSF).

**Step 2 — Deconvolution Requirement:** To recover a sharp image, the $\frac{1}{r}$ blur must be deconvolved. In the frequency domain, this PSF becomes $\frac{1}{\vert \omega \vert}$, so the deconvolution filter is $\vert \omega \vert$ — the **ramp filter**.

**Step 3 — FBP Formula:**

$$
f(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(\omega, \theta) \, \vert \omega \vert \, e^{j2\pi\omega(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega \right] d\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Implementation:** The ramp filter is applied as a 1D convolution along each projection. To suppress high-frequency noise amplification, windowed versions are used (Hamming, Hann, Shepp-Logan).

**Advantages over Simple Back-Projection (3 marks):**
1. **Sharp edge recovery** due to ramp filter deconvolution.
2. **Quantitative accuracy** — pixel values reflect true attenuation coefficients (Hounsfield Units).
3. **Computational efficiency** — single convolution per angle, suitable for clinical real-time reconstruction.

**(Marks distribution: 3 for derivation, 2 for ramp filter explanation, 2 for advantages)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

### (a) State and prove the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem. Discuss its implications in medical image digitization. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** A band-limited continuous signal $x_c(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its discrete samples $x(nT)$ if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s = 1/T$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

**Proof Sketch (5 marks):**
- Sampling is modeled as multiplication by a Dirac comb: $x_s(t) = x_c(t) \cdot \sum_n \delta(t - nT)$.
- Fourier Transform yields $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_k X_c(f - k f_s)$ — periodic spectrum.
- No overlap condition: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- Reconstruction via ideal low-pass filter: $x_c(t) = \sum_n x(nT) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)$.

**Implications in Medical Imaging (2 marks):**
- CT detector pitch $\Delta x$ determines maximum resolvable spatial frequency $f_{max} = \frac{1}{2\Delta x}$.
- MRI: receiver bandwidth must exceed twice the maximum frequency in the signal.
- Ultrasound: A/D sampling must be at least twice the highest RF frequency (typically 40 MHz for 20 MHz probes).

---

### (b) Compare Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) with Iterative Reconstruction (IR) techniques like ART and SIRT in CT. Discuss when each is preferred. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | FBP | Iterative (ART/SIRT) |
|---------|-----|----------------------|
| Speed | Very fast (analytical) | Slower (iterative) |
| Noise handling | Moderate | Excellent |
| Limited-angle data | Poor | Robust |
| Mathematical basis | Fourier Slice Theorem | Algebraic / Statistical |
| Dose reduction | Limited | Significant (30–60%) |
| Hardware | CPU/GPU friendly | GPU-accelerated preferred |

**Discussion (4 marks):**
- **FBP preferred** when: data is complete (full $360°$ or $180°+$ fan), dose is standard, real-time reconstruction is needed (e.g., interventional CT).
- **Iterative preferred** when: low-dose scans (e.g., pediatric, screening), limited-angle geometry (dental CBCT, C-arm), or when metal artifacts are problematic.
- Modern hybrid approaches (e.g., **ASIR, SAFIRE, ASiR-V, TrueFidelity, AiCE**) blend model-based iterative reconstruction with deep learning priors for optimal speed-quality trade-off.

**(Marks distribution: 3 for comparison table, 4 for discussion)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Radon Transform" with "Fourier Transform":** They are related (via the Central Slice Theorem) but fundamentally different. Radon is a *spatial line integral*; Fourier is a *frequency decomposition*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Jacobian $\vert \omega \vert$:** When converting 2D inverse FT to polar coordinates in FBP, the $\vert \omega \vert$ factor is **mandatory**. Omitting it leads to incorrect reconstruction.
> 3. **Angle Range Error:** Projections are unique only over $\theta \in [0, \pi)$, not $[0, 2\pi)$. Integrating over the full circle is a common mistake.
> 4. **Sampling Theorem Misapplication:** The Nyquist criterion applies to *band-limited* signals. Real tissue signals are not strictly band-limited, so practical systems use *oversampling* (e.g., 2.5× to 3× the theoretical limit).
> 5. **Mixing Continuous and Discrete Notation:** Examiners deduct marks if you switch between integrals ($\int$) and summations ($\sum$) without clear justification.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Radon Transform** = line integral of an object; central to CT acquisition.
- **Central Slice Theorem** = bridge between projection space and Fourier space.
- **Simple Back-Projection** smears the image; needs $\frac{1}{r}$ deconvolution.
- **Ramp Filter** $\vert \omega \vert$ deconvolves the blur, yielding FBP.
- **Nyquist Criterion** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ is the sampling foundation.
- **Iterative Reconstruction (ART/SIRT)** is preferred in low-dose and limited-angle CT.
- **FBP** is fast, analytical, and the clinical workhorse for standard CT.
- **Quantization** with $b$-bit ADC gives $2^b$ intensity levels; CT uses **12-bit**, MRI uses **16-bit**.
- **MTF** characterizes spatial resolution; **SNR** characterizes noise.
- **K-space** (MRI) and **Sinogram** (CT) are the two key raw data domains.
- The Jacobian $\vert \omega \vert$ in FBP is non-negotiable; the angle range is $[0, \pi)$.
- Modern AI reconstruction (e.g., deep learning priors) is replacing classical regularization in cutting-edge systems, but the underlying physics (Radon, Central Slice) remains the same.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Storage, Retrieval, and Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET636 - BIOMEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Image Storage, Retrieval, and Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Image Storage, Retrieval, and Communication

In the context of **Biomedical Imaging**, the triad of **Image Storage, Retrieval, and Communication** refers to the integrated digital infrastructure — primarily implemented through the **Picture Archiving and Communication System (PACS)** — used to acquire, archive, transmit, and display medical images across healthcare enterprises. The foundational standards governing this infrastructure are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System):** A medically oriented, computer-controlled system designed for the storage, retrieval, distribution, and presentation of medical images, operating in conformance with the **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine):** A globally adopted, vendor-independent standard (currently maintained by the **NEMA — National Electrical Manufacturers Association**) that defines the formats, protocols, and services for the exchange of medical images and related metadata.

> [!NOTE]
> **HL7 (Health Level Seven):** A complementary standard governing the textual/clinical data exchange (e.g., patient demographics, orders, reports) between hospital information systems. HL7 and DICOM together enable the **IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise)** framework.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Medical Library"

Imagine a **centralized, fully digital smart library** for a hospital:

- **The Library Building** = The **PACS Server / Archive** (stores millions of images)
- **The Cataloging System** = The **DICOM Header** (tags every image with patient name, modality, date, study UID)
- **The Librarians** = **DICOM Application Entities** (modalities, workstations, archives)
- **The Wi-Fi / Internet** = The **Hospital LAN / WAN** (using TCP/IP as the transport layer)
- **The Reading Rooms** = **Diagnostic Workstations** (where radiologists view images)
- **The Lending Process** = **WADO (Web Access to DICOM Objects)** / **C-MOVE / C-GET** (retrieval protocols)

Just as a librarian instantly retrieves a specific book using its catalog ID, PACS instantly retrieves a specific medical image using the **Study Instance UID (0020,000D)** and **Series Instance UID (0020,000E)** — eliminating physical film jackets, light boxes, and manual searching.

### 1.3 Why This Matters in Modern Healthcare

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - A modern CT scan produces **~2,000 slices × 16-bit depth × 512 × 512 pixels ≈ 1 GB** of raw data.
> - A busy hospital generates **10–20 TB of imaging data per year**.
> - Without **standardized storage + high-speed communication**, this digital explosion would be unmanageable.
> - Standards-based communication enables **teleradiology**, **multi-hospital image sharing**, and **AI-driven diagnostics**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of the Image Management Ecosystem

#### Pillar 1 — Image Acquisition (Modalities)
- Modalities (CT, MRI, X-Ray, US, PET) generate images in **vendor-specific raw formats**.
- **DICOM Conformance Statement**: Every modality vendor publishes this document declaring which DICOM Service Classes (SOP Classes) their device supports.
- Modality acts as a **DICOM Service Class User (SCU)** — it pushes images to the archive.

#### Pillar 2 — Image Storage (Archive)
- **Short-term storage**: Local disk arrays (RAID-5/6, SSD) — fast access for active studies.
- **Long-term storage**: Tiered architecture:
  - **Tier 1 (Online)**: Disk-based, < 1 second access — used for recent studies (~3–6 months).
  - **Tier 2 (Nearline)**: Optical jukeboxes / enterprise tape (LTO — Linear Tape-Open) — minutes access.
  - **Tier 3 (Offline)**: Deep archive tapes — hours access, lowest cost per GB.
- **Storage Redundancy**: **RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks)** levels ensure fault tolerance.
  - RAID-1: Mirroring (1:1 redundancy)
  - RAID-5: Striping + single parity (N-1 usable capacity)
  - RAID-6: Striping + double parity (N-2 usable capacity)

> [!NOTE]
> Modern PACS increasingly use **cloud-based archives** (AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCP) with **WORM (Write Once Read Many)** compliance for legal medical record retention.

#### Pillar 3 — Image Retrieval (Query/Retrieve)
DICOM defines **C-FIND**, **C-MOVE**, and **C-GET** as the retrieval service classes:

| Service | Role | Direction |
|---------|------|-----------|
| **C-FIND** | Query / Search | Workstation → Archive (returns matching record list) |
| **C-MOVE** | Third-party retrieve | Workstation asks Archive to send images to a target AE |
| **C-GET** | Direct retrieve | Workstation pulls images directly from Archive |
| **WADO** | Web-based retrieve | Browser fetches images via HTTP/HTTPS |
| **WADO-RS** | RESTful retrieve | Modern REST API for web/mobile clients |
| **QIDO-RS** | Query via REST | Search DICOM objects using HTTP |
| **STOW-RS** | Store via REST | Upload DICOM objects using HTTP |

#### Pillar 4 — Image Communication (Networking)
- **Transport Layer**: **TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** — reliable, connection-oriented.
- **Upper Layer Protocol (DICOM UL)**: Adds association negotiation (Presentation Contexts, Transfer Syntaxes).
- **Port**: DICOM uses **port 104** (and port 11112 for WADO).
- **Bandwidth Requirements**:
  - Standard hospital LAN: **1–10 Gbps** Ethernet.
  - Teleradiology WAN: **50–500 Mbps** typical, often compressed.
  - Wireless inside hospital: **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)** for mobile image review on tablets.

### 2.2 The DICOM Standard — Structural Anatomy

A **DICOM file** consists of two parts:

1. **DICOM File Meta Information (Group 0002)** — File Meta Information Version, Transfer Syntax UID, Media Storage SOP Class UID.
2. **DICOM Dataset** — A stream of **Data Elements** (Tag, VR, Length, Value).

**DICOM Tag = (Group Number, Element Number)** — each tag is a 4-byte hexadecimal identifier, e.g., $(0010,0010)$ = Patient's Name.

**Information Object Definitions (IODs)** — define *what* data is stored. Examples:
- **CR Image IOD** (Computed Radiography)
- **CT Image IOD**
- **MR Image IOD**
- **US Image IOD** (Ultrasound)
- **PET Image IOD**

**Service Classes** — define *what operations* can be performed:
- **Storage Service Class**
- **Query/Retrieve Service Class**
- **Modality Worklist (MWL) Service Class**
- **Print Management Service Class**
- **Storage Commitment Service Class** (confirms archive responsibility)

### 2.3 Image Compression — Lossless vs. Lossy

| Feature | Lossless Compression | Lossy Compression |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Technique** | Run-Length, Huffman, LZW, JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000 Lossless | JPEG, JPEG 2000, Wavelet, MPEG |
| **Achievable Ratio** | **2:1 to 3:1** typically | **10:1 to 30:1** possible |
| **Information Preserved** | Pixel-perfect reconstruction | Diagnostic information MAY be preserved |
| **Clinical Use** | Primary diagnosis (always preferred) | Archival, teaching, teleradiology (with caution) |
| **Standard in DICOM** | JPEG Lossless, JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000 Lossless | JPEG Baseline, JPEG 2000 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Lossy compression (e.g., JPEG at ratios > 10:1) is **NOT acceptable for primary diagnostic interpretation** in many jurisdictions, including by the **American College of Radiology (ACR)** and **Royal College of Radiologists (RCR)** guidelines. Always mention this distinction in answers.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula set** for any PACS / DICOM / storage numerical problem in KTU ESE.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Equation / Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Values |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Uncompressed Image Size | $S = W \times H \times B \times N_{frames} \times N_{bits}$ bytes | $W$ = width, $H$ = height, $B$ = bit depth / 8 | — |
| 2 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Dimensionless | Lossless $\approx 2$–$3$, Lossy $\approx 10$–$30$ |
| 3 | Percentage Space Saved | $\%\,saved = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100$ | Percent | — |
| 4 | Achievable Bit Rate (Compressed) | $R_c = \dfrac{S_{original} \times 8}{CR \times T}$ | bits per second (bps) | — |
| 5 | Channel Capacity Required | $C = N_{avg} \times R_c \times SF$ | bps | $SF$ = safety factor 1.2–1.5 |
| 6 | Transmission Time | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{S_{compressed} \times 8}{B_{w}}$ | seconds | $B_w$ = link bandwidth in bps |
| 7 | RAID-5 Usable Capacity | $C_{usable} = (N - 1) \times C_{disk}$ | Bytes / TB | $N$ = number of disks |
| 8 | RAID-6 Usable Capacity | $C_{usable} = (N - 2) \times C_{disk}$ | Bytes / TB | — |
| 9 | Effective Storage per Patient (Annual) | $E_{pt} = \sum_{i} \left(\dfrac{S_i \times F_i}{CR_i}\right)$ | GB / patient / year | — |
| 10 | Information Entropy (Shannon) | $H = -\sum_{k=1}^{n} p_k \log_2(p_k)$ | bits per pixel | Bounds the lossless compression limit |
| 11 | Mean Square Error (Compression Quality) | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{WH}\sum_{x,y}\left[I(x,y) - \hat{I}(x,y)\right]^2$ | squared intensity units | — |
| 12 | Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{(2^n - 1)^2}{MSE}\right)$ | decibels (dB) | $> 40$ dB = high quality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always quote **bit depth** carefully: 8-bit = **1 byte**, 12-bit = **1.5 bytes**, 16-bit = **2 bytes** per pixel. CT/MRI typically use **16-bit** grayscale.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uncompressed Image Size for a Multi-Frame 3D Medical Study

> **Problem:** A chest CT acquisition produces $N_{frames} = 1800$ slices. Each slice is reconstructed at a matrix size of $W = 512$ pixels × $H = 512$ pixels with a $B = 16$-bit grayscale depth. Calculate the total uncompressed data volume.

**Step 1 — Express the size per single frame (slice).**

$$
S_{1\,frame} = W \times H \times \dfrac{B}{8}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
S_{1\,frame} = 512 \times 512 \times \dfrac{16}{8}
$$

Evaluate the pixel count first:

$$
S_{1\,frame} = 262144 \text{ pixels} \times 2 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{1\,frame} = 524288 \text{ bytes per slice}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to more intuitive units (KB, MB).**

Divide by $1024$ to convert to KiB (note: image storage contexts frequently use $1024$ rather than $1000$):

$$
S_{1\,frame} = \dfrac{524288}{1024} \text{ KiB} = 512 \text{ KiB}
$$

Divide again by $1024$:

$$
S_{1\,frame} = \dfrac{512}{1024} \text{ MiB} = 0.5 \text{ MiB per slice}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by total number of frames.**

$$
S_{total} = S_{1\,frame} \times N_{frames}
$$

$$
S_{total} = 0.5 \text{ MiB} \times 1800
$$

$$
S_{total} = 900 \text{ MiB}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to GiB (gigabytes, binary).**

$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{900}{1024} \text{ GiB} \approx 0.879 \text{ GiB}
$$

**Final Answer:** $S_{total} \approx 900\ \text{MiB} \approx 0.879\ \text{GiB}$ of uncompressed DICOM data.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **contrast-enhanced chest CT with 2 phases**, double this value → $\approx 1.76\ \text{GiB}$ per study. A busy department performing 50 such CTs/day generates **~33 GiB/day**, or roughly **12 TiB/year** — justifying tiered storage architectures.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compression Ratio and Percentage Space Saved

> **Problem:** The CT study above is archived using **JPEG 2000 Lossless compression** with $CR = 2.5$. Determine: (a) compressed size, (b) percentage space saved.

**(a) Compressed Size Calculation.**

By definition of compression ratio:

$$
CR = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; S_{compressed} = \dfrac{S_{original}}{CR}
$$

Substitute $S_{original} = 900\ \text{MiB}$ and $CR = 2.5$:

$$
S_{compressed} = \dfrac{900}{2.5}
$$

$$
S_{compressed} = 360\ \text{MiB}
$$

**(b) Percentage Space Saved Calculation.**

The percentage saved is the complement of the inverse compression ratio, scaled to percent:

$$
\%\,\text{saved} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{CR} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\%\,\text{saved} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.5} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\%\,\text{saved} = (1 - 0.4) \times 100
$$

$$
\%\,\text{saved} = 0.6 \times 100 = 60\,\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $S_{compressed} = 360\ \text{MiB}$, and a **60 %** reduction in storage footprint is achieved.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transmission Time Across a Hospital LAN

> **Problem:** A radiologist in a remote reading room requests the compressed CT study ($S_{compressed} = 360\ \text{MiB}$) over a hospital LAN with effective bandwidth $B_w = 1\ \text{Gbps}$. Calculate the transmission time. Assume negligible protocol overhead.

**Step 1 — Convert compressed size from MiB to bits.**

First convert MiB to MB (megabytes, using $1\ \text{MiB} = 2^{20}\ \text{bytes}$):

$$
S_{bits} = S_{compressed} \times 8 \times 2^{20}
$$

Substitute $S_{compressed} = 360$:

$$
S_{bits} = 360 \times 8 \times 1{,}048{,}576
$$

$$
S_{bits} = 360 \times 8{,}388{,}608 = 3{,}019{,}898{,}880\ \text{bits}
$$

$$
S_{bits} \approx 3.02 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert bandwidth to bits per second.**

$$
B_w = 1\ \text{Gbps} = 10^{9}\ \text{bits/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute transmission time.**

$$
T_{tx} = \dfrac{S_{bits}}{B_w}
$$

$$
T_{tx} = \dfrac{3.02 \times 10^{9}}{10^{9}}
$$

$$
T_{tx} \approx 3.02\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{tx} \approx 3.02\ \text{s}$ — illustrating why **gigabit LANs are mandatory** in modern imaging departments.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — RAID-5 Capacity for an Archive Tier

> **Problem:** A hospital procures $N = 8$ enterprise SAS disks, each $C_{disk} = 4\ \text{TiB}$, configured in RAID-5 for short-term archive. Determine the usable capacity.

**Step 1 — Apply the RAID-5 usable capacity formula.**

In RAID-5, the equivalent of one disk is consumed by parity (rotating parity across disks):

$$
C_{usable} = (N - 1) \times C_{disk}
$$

Substitute:

$$
C_{usable} = (8 - 1) \times 4\ \text{TiB}
$$

$$
C_{usable} = 7 \times 4\ \text{TiB} = 28\ \text{TiB}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the overhead percentage.**

$$
\%\,\text{overhead} = \dfrac{1}{N} \times 100 = \dfrac{1}{8} \times 100 = 12.5\,\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $C_{usable} = 28\ \text{TiB}$ with **12.5 %** storage overhead. RAID-5 tolerates the failure of any **one** disk.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Annual Storage Growth Forecast

> **Problem:** A 500-bed hospital performs the following average annual studies:
> - 8000 CT scans @ 900 MiB each (uncompressed), compressed at CR = 2.5
> - 6000 MRI scans @ 250 MiB each (uncompressed), compressed at CR = 3
> - 25,000 X-ray CR studies @ 25 MiB each (uncompressed), compressed at CR = 4
>
> Compute the annual compressed storage requirement.

**Step 1 — CT contribution.**

$$
E_{CT} = 8000 \times \dfrac{900}{2.5} = 8000 \times 360 = 2{,}880{,}000\ \text{MiB}
$$

Convert to TiB (using $1\ \text{TiB} = 2^{20}\ \text{MiB} = 1{,}048{,}576\ \text{MiB}$):

$$
E_{CT} = \dfrac{2{,}880{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 2.746\ \text{TiB}
$$

**Step 2 — MRI contribution.**

$$
E_{MRI} = 6000 \times \dfrac{250}{3} = 6000 \times 83.33 \approx 500{,}000\ \text{MiB}
$$

Convert:

$$
E_{MRI} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 0.477\ \text{TiB}
$$

**Step 3 — X-Ray (CR) contribution.**

$$
E_{CR} = 25{,}000 \times \dfrac{25}{4} = 25{,}000 \times 6.25 = 156{,}250\ \text{MiB}
$$

Convert:

$$
E_{CR} = \dfrac{156{,}250}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 0.149\ \text{TiB}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum across modalities.**

$$
E_{total} = 2.746 + 0.477 + 0.149 = 3.372\ \text{TiB / year}
$$

**Step 5 — Add 30 % headroom for indexing, metadata, DICOMDIR, logs, and growth margin.**

$$
E_{provisioned} = 3.372 \times 1.3 \approx 4.38\ \text{TiB / year}
$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **4.4 TiB/year** must be provisioned in compressed primary archive — illustrating why even mid-sized hospitals need **petascale archives** within a decade.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Compression Technique Comparison

| Technique | Standard Family | Compression Type | Typical Ratio | Diagnostic Safe? | Complexity |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|---------------|------------------|-----------|
| Run-Length Encoding (RLE) | DICOM-native | Lossless | 1.5 : 1 to 2 : 1 | Yes | Low |
| Huffman + LZW | DICOM-native | Lossless | 2 : 1 to 3 : 1 | Yes | Low |
| JPEG-LS | ISO 14495 | Lossless | 2.5 : 1 to 3 : 1 | Yes | Medium |
| JPEG 2000 Lossless | ISO 15444-1 | Lossless | 2.5 : 1 to 3.5 : 1 | Yes | High |
| JPEG Baseline | ISO 10918 | Lossy | 10 : 1 to 20 : 1 | Conditional | Medium |
| JPEG 2000 Lossy | ISO 15444-1 | Lossy | 20 : 1 to 40 : 1 | Conditional | High |
| Wavelet (SPIHT, EZW) | Research / Vendor | Both | 10 : 1 to 30 : 1 | Conditional | High |
| MPEG-2 / H.264 | DICOM Video | Lossy | 50 : 1 to 200 : 1 | For US video only | Medium |

### 4.2 DICOM Communication Sequence — Association, Transfer, Release

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Mod as Modality SCU
    participant Arc as PACS Archive SCP

    Mod->>Arc: A_ASSOCIATE_RQ (Presentation Contexts, Transfer Syntaxes)
    Arc-->>Mod: A_ASSOCIATE_AC (Accepted Contexts)
    Note over Mod,Arc: TCP Port 104 Established

    Mod->>Arc: C_STORE_RQ (DICOM Dataset, e.g., CT Image)
    Arc-->>Mod: C_STORE_RSP (Status: Success 0x0000)

    Mod->>Arc: C_STORE_RQ (Next Slice)
    Arc-->>Mod: C_STORE_RSP (Success)

    Mod->>Arc: C_ECHO_RQ (Verification / Keep-Alive)
    Arc-->>Mod: C_ECHO_RSP (Success)

    Mod->>Arc: A_RELEASE_RQ
    Arc-->>Mod: A_RELEASE_RP
    Note over Mod,Arc: TCP Connection Closed
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define PACS and list its main components. Briefly explain its role in a modern hospital.**

**Model Answer:**

**PACS** (Picture Archiving and Communication System) is a computer-based medical imaging technology used to **store, retrieve, distribute, and display** digital medical images in conformance with the DICOM standard.

**Main Components of PACS:**
1. **Image Acquisition Modalities** (CT, MRI, X-Ray, US, PET) — generate images
2. **PACS Server / Controller** — manages workflow, routing, and queries
3. **Archive Subsystem** — short-term (RAID) and long-term (tape/cloud) storage
4. **Communication Network** — TCP/IP LAN/WAN infrastructure
5. **Display Workstations** — diagnostic and clinical review stations
6. **Integration Gateways** — connect with HIS / RIS / EHR

**Role in Modern Hospital:**
- Eliminates physical film, light boxes, and lost jackets
- Enables **instant image access** across departments
- Supports **teleradiology** and **AI-assisted diagnosis**
- Integrates with **HL7 / IHE** for complete electronic patient record
- Reduces long-term storage cost per study

*(3 marks — concise definition + 4–5 components + 1–2 role points)*

---

#### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between lossless and lossy image compression. State one clinical limitation of lossy compression.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lossless Compression | Lossy Compression |
|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Reconstruction** | Bit-perfect | Approximate |
| **Ratio** | 2:1 to 3:1 | 10:1 to 30:1 |
| **Information** | Preserved | Partially discarded |
| **Algorithm Examples** | JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000 Lossless, RLE, Huffman | JPEG Baseline, JPEG 2000 Lossy, Wavelet |
| **Use Case** | Primary diagnosis | Archival, teaching, teleradiology |

**Clinical Limitation of Lossy Compression:**
At high compression ratios (>10:1 for JPEG Baseline), subtle diagnostic features such as **small pulmonary nodules, microcalcifications in mammography, and low-contrast lesions in CT/MRI** may be irreversibly degraded. Hence, lossy compression is **not acceptable for primary diagnostic interpretation** as per ACR / RCR guidelines.

*(3 marks — clean comparison table + one clinical caveat)*

---

### Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### ⭐ Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **DICOM standard** in detail. Explain the structure of a DICOM file, the concept of **Information Object Definitions (IODs)**, **Service Classes**, and **Transfer Syntaxes**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a PACS system**. Discuss the role of **HIS / RIS integration** and the use of **DICOM query/retrieve services** in clinical workflow. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:**
DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) is a globally adopted, vendor-independent standard developed and maintained by **NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association)** that defines the formats, communication protocols, and services for medical imaging information exchange.

**DICOM File Structure [2 marks]:**

A DICOM file comprises:

1. **Preamble (128 bytes)** — application profile / reserved.
2. **DICM Prefix (4 bytes)** — the literal text "DICM" signaling DICOM format.
3. **File Meta Information (Group 0002 elements)** — contains:
   - (0002,0000) File Meta Information Group Length
   - (0002,0001) File Meta Information Version
   - (0002,0002) **Media Storage SOP Class UID**
   - (0002,0003) **Media Storage SOP Instance UID**
   - (0002,0010) **Transfer Syntax UID**
   - (0002,0012) Implementation Class UID
4. **Dataset** — sequence of Data Elements (Tag, VR, Length, Value).

Each **Data Element** has:
- **Tag** = (Group, Element) — 4 bytes, e.g., (0010,0010) = Patient's Name
- **VR (Value Representation)** — type: CS, DS, LO, PN, US, etc.
- **Length** — explicit or implicit
- **Value Field** — actual data

**Information Object Definitions (IODs) [2 marks]:**
IODs are **abstract data models** that define the set of attributes belonging to a real-world entity (e.g., a CT image, an MR image, a patient). Each IOD specifies:
- **Information Entity (IE) Modules**: Patient, Study, Series, Equipment, Image
- Example: **CT Image IOD** includes Patient module, Study module, Series module, Equipment module, Image module (with rows/columns, pixel spacing, slice thickness, kVp, mA).

**Service Classes [1 mark]:**
Service Classes define the **operations** applied to IODs:
- **Storage Service Class** — push images (C-STORE)
- **Query/Retrieve Service Class** — search (C-FIND), fetch (C-MOVE/C-GET)
- **Modality Worklist Service Class** — schedule patients (MWL)
- **Storage Commitment Service Class** — confirm archive responsibility
- **Print Management Service Class** — print to DICOM printer

**Transfer Syntaxes [1 mark]:**
Transfer Syntaxes define the **encoding rules** for the dataset (byte order endianness, implicit vs explicit VR, compression applied). Examples:
- **1.2.840.10008.1.2** — Implicit VR Little Endian (default)
- **1.2.840.10008.1.2.1** — Explicit VR Little Endian
- **1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.70** — JPEG Lossless
- **1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.90** — JPEG 2000 Lossless
- **1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.91** — JPEG 2000 Lossy

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**PACS Architecture Diagram [3 marks] — see SECTION 4.1 above.**

**Key Subsystems [2 marks]:**

1. **Acquisition Subsystem** — Modalities (CT/MRI/CR/US/PET) acting as **DICOM SCU** pushing C-STORE messages to the PACS server.

2. **PACS Server / Controller** — Receives, validates, and routes incoming studies. Performs **DICOM conformance negotiation** (association establishment over TCP port 104).

3. **Archive Subsystem** — Two-tier storage:
   - **Tier 1**: Online RAID-5/6 disk array (fast access, < 1 s)
   - **Tier 2/3**: Near-line tape / cloud archive (long-term retention, minutes access)

4. **Database / Indexer** — Maintains DICOMDIR and database of studies (Patient ID, Study UID, accession number) for fast lookup.

5. **Display Workstations** — Diagnostic (4MP grayscale mammography monitors) and clinical review stations.

6. **Network Infrastructure** — Hospital LAN (10 Gbps backbone), firewalls, WAN for teleradiology.

**HIS / RIS Integration [1 mark]:**
- **HL7 messaging** flows patient demographics, orders, and reports between HIS / RIS and PACS.
- The **Modality Worklist (MWL)** server in RIS feeds scheduled patient information to modalities, avoiding manual entry errors.
- **IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise)** profiles such as *Scheduled Workflow (SWF)* orchestrate the end-to-end chain.

**DICOM Query/Retrieve in Workflow [1 mark]:**
- Radiologist workstation issues **C-FIND** to the PACS archive to locate a study (e.g., "all CT Chest studies for patient P123 in last 7 days").
- Archive returns a list of matching studies.
- Radiologist selects a study and issues **C-MOVE** (or C-GET) to retrieve the full DICOM dataset.
- The images stream to the diagnostic workstation for viewing, processing, and reporting.

---

#### ⭐ Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain **image compression techniques** used in medical imaging. Compare lossless and lossy methods with suitable examples, and derive an expression for **compression ratio and percentage space saved**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chest CT generates **1500 slices** of matrix size **$512 \times 512$ pixels** at **16-bit depth**. Compute: (i) uncompressed study size, (ii) compressed size using **JPEG 2000 Lossless** with **CR = 2.8**, (iii) percentage storage saved, and (iv) transmission time over a **500 Mbps** link. Comment on the impact of lossy compression (CR = 20) on diagnostic quality and legal acceptability. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Need for Compression [1 mark]:**
Modern medical imaging generates enormous data volumes (e.g., a single multi-phase CT study ≈ 1 GiB). Compression reduces storage cost and accelerates network transmission, which is critical for teleradiology and workflow efficiency.

**Lossless Compression [2 marks]:**
Lossless techniques allow **bit-perfect reconstruction** of the original image.

| Technique | Principle | Typical Ratio |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** | Encodes runs of identical pixel values | 1.5:1 to 2:1 |
| **Huffman Coding** | Variable-length codes for symbol probabilities | 2:1 to 3:1 |
| **LZW (Lempel-Ziv-Welch)** | Dictionary-based string replacement | 2:1 to 3:1 |
| **JPEG-LS** | Predictive coding + Golomb-Rice entropy coding | 2.5:1 to 3:1 |
| **JPEG 2000 Lossless** | Integer-reversible wavelet + arithmetic coding | 2.5:1 to 3.5:1 |

**Lossy Compression [2 marks]:**
Lossy techniques achieve **higher ratios by discarding perceptually/diagnostically less significant information**.

| Technique | Principle | Typical Ratio |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **JPEG Baseline (DCT)** | 8×8 block DCT + quantization | 10:1 to 20:1 |
| **JPEG 2000 Lossy** | Discrete Wavelet + quantization | 20:1 to 40:1 |
| **Wavelet (SPIHT, EZW)** | Sub-band coding + zero-tree | 10:1 to 30:1 |
| **MPEG / H.264** | Inter-frame motion prediction | 50:1 to 200:1 |

**Mathematical Expressions [2 marks]:**

The **compression ratio** is defined as:

$$
CR = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}
$$

The **percentage space saved** is:

$$
\%\,saved = \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{CR} \right) \times 100
$$

The theoretical **lower bound on lossless compression** is given by **Shannon's entropy**:

$$
H = -\sum_{k=1}^{n} p_k \log_2 p_k \;\;\text{bits/symbol}
$$

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- $N_{frames} = 1500$ slices
- $W \times H = 512 \times 512$ pixels
- $B = 16$ bits = 2 bytes per pixel
- Lossless case: $CR_{LL} = 2.8$
- Lossy case: $CR_{L} = 20$
- Bandwidth: $B_w = 500\ \text{Mbps}$

**(i) Uncompressed study size [2 marks]:**

Per-slice size:

$$
S_{1\,frame} = 512 \times 512 \times 2 = 524288 \text{ bytes} = 0.5\ \text{MiB}
$$

Total study size:

$$
S_{original} = 0.5 \times 1500 = 750\ \text{MiB}
$$

Convert to GiB:

$$
S_{original} = \dfrac{750}{1024} \approx 0.732\ \text{GiB}
$$

**(ii) Compressed size with CR = 2.8 [1 mark]:**

$$
S_{comp,\,LL} = \dfrac{750}{2.8} \approx 267.86\ \text{MiB}
$$

**(iii) Percentage storage saved [1 mark]:**

$$
\%\,\text{saved} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.8} \right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.357) \times 100 = 64.3\,\%
$$

**(iv) Transmission time over 500 Mbps link [2 marks]:**

Convert size to bits:

$$
S_{bits} = 267.86\ \text{MiB} \times 2^{20} \times 8 \approx 267.86 \times 1{,}048{,}576 \times 8 \approx 2.247 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bits}
$$

Transmission time:

$$
T_{tx} = \dfrac{S_{bits}}{B_w} = \dfrac{2.247 \times 10^{9}}{5 \times 10^{8}} \approx 4.49\ \text{seconds}
$$

**(v) Lossy compression (CR = 20) — diagnostic and legal impact [1 mark]:**

$$
S_{comp,\,L} = \dfrac{750}{20} = 37.5\ \text{MiB}
$$

With $CR = 20$, **JPEG Baseline** discards high-frequency DCT coefficients that may carry subtle diagnostic features (e.g., small lung nodules, microcalcifications, low-contrast lesions). Most regulatory bodies (**ACR, RCR, RSNA**) and **Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR)** guidelines **do not permit lossy compression for primary diagnostic interpretation**. Such compressed images may be acceptable for **archival backups, teaching files, or remote consultations where original DICOM is also available**, but never as the sole diagnostic record. Misuse may have **medico-legal consequences** and breach **DICOM PS 3.4 conformance** obligations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always quote bit depth correctly** — 12-bit images use 12 bits per pixel (not 1.5 bytes unless explicitly computed).
> 2. **CR is defined as original ÷ compressed**, never the inverse. Writing $CR = S_c/S_o$ will cost you **2 full marks**.
> 3. **For RAID problems**: do not forget the parity disk in RAID-5 — usable capacity is $(N-1) \times C_{disk}$.
> 4. **DICOM tag notation**: examiners expect the format **(Group, Element)** in hex, e.g., (0010,0010), not decimal.
> 5. **Lossy compression is unacceptable for primary diagnosis** — failing to mention this limitation typically loses 1 mark in any compression-based question.
> 6. **Don't confuse DICOM with HL7** — DICOM handles imaging data; HL7 handles textual/clinical data. Many students mix them up.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 1 (Image Storage, Retrieval, and Communication)**

- ✅ **PACS** = digital system for medical image **storage, retrieval, communication, and display**.
- ✅ **DICOM** = the universal medical imaging standard (NEMA-maintained, versioned as "PS 3.x").
- ✅ **HL7** = textual/clinical data exchange; **IHE** = integration profile framework.
- ✅ DICOM file = **Preamble + DICM Prefix + File Meta (Group 0002) + Dataset** of tagged elements.
- ✅ DICOM tag format = **(Group Number, Element Number)** in 4-byte hex.
- ✅ **IOD** = what data is stored; **Service Class** = what operations are done; **Transfer Syntax** = how data is encoded.
- ✅ DICOM default port = **TCP/104**; **WADO** uses HTTP/HTTPS (port 80/443).
- ✅ Service operations: **C-STORE** (push), **C-FIND** (search), **C-MOVE** (third-party pull), **C-GET** (direct pull), **C-ECHO** (verification), **A-RELEASE** (terminate).
- ✅ **MWL (Modality Worklist)** avoids manual entry of patient demographics into modalities.
- ✅ **Storage tiers**: Tier 1 (online RAID disk, fast) → Tier 2 (nearline tape, minutes) → Tier 3 (offline / cloud, hours).
- ✅ **RAID-5 usable** = $(N-1) \times C_{disk}$ (one parity disk); **RAID-6** = $(N-2) \times C_{disk}$ (double parity).
- ✅ **Lossless compression** = bit-perfect, 2:1 to 3:1 ratio, used for primary diagnosis (JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000 Lossless).
- ✅ **Lossy compression** = approximation, 10:1 to 30:1+ ratio, NOT acceptable for primary diagnosis (per ACR / RCR guidelines).
- ✅ **Compression Ratio** $CR = S_{original}/S_{compressed}$; **% saved** = $(1 - 1/CR) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Shannon entropy** $H = -\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$ bounds the lossless compression limit.
- ✅ **PSNR** = $10 \log_{10}[(2^n - 1)^2 / MSE]$ — used to benchmark lossy reconstruction quality; > 40 dB is generally acceptable.
- ✅ Hospital LAN is typically **1–10 Gbps**; a 1 GiB study traverses in **~1–8 seconds** over gigabit links.
- ✅ Annual imaging data growth for a mid-sized hospital ≈ **5–20 TiB/year compressed**.
- ✅ **DICOMDIR** = the index file (like a directory) that lists all DICOM files on a storage medium.
- ✅ **Storage Commitment** SOP class = archive confirms to the modality that the data is safely stored.
- ✅ **WADO-RS / QIDO-RS / STOW-RS** = RESTful modern web services (DICOM PS 3.18) replacing classical DIMSE.
- ✅ **Cloud PACS** must implement **WORM** compliance, **HIPAA / DISHA / GDPR** data protection, and **DICOM conformance**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Biomedical Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET636 - BIOMEDICAL IMAGING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Applications of Biomedical Imaging # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Biomedical Imaging** is the multidisciplinary field of science and engineering concerned with the visual representation, characterization, and quantification of biological structures and functions at scales ranging from whole organs down to molecular and cellular levels, using physical phenomena such as X-ray attenuation, magnetic resonance, ultrasonic reflection, radioactive decay, and optical interaction with tissue. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAET636)** syllabus, biomedical imaging applications span the entire clinical workflow — from **screening** and **diagnosis** to **interventional guidance**, **therapy monitoring**, and **research**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The applications focus on understanding *which imaging modality* is best suited for a *specific clinical question*, *organ system*, and *patient condition*. The choice of modality is governed by the **ALARA principle** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) for radiation dose and by tissue-specific contrast mechanisms. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **biomedical imaging system as a specialized "camera"** — but one that does not just record visible light. Instead, it "listens" to different physical "languages" the body speaks: - **X-rays** → the body's *shadow language* (dense bones cast darker shadows). - **MRI** → the body's *radio broadcast* (hydrogen atoms in water sing back when pulsed). - **Ultrasound** → the body's *echo* (sound bouncing off organ walls, like a bat's sonar). - **PET/SPECT** → the body's *glowing tracer* (a radioactive "GPS tag" lights up where metabolism is high). - **Optical imaging** → the body's *visible surface* (light penetrating shallow tissue layers). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine diagnosing a car problem. An **X-ray** is like checking for cracks in the chassis, an **MRI** is like tapping every panel and listening for hollow spots, an **Ultrasound** is like rolling the car over rumble strips to feel tire wear, and a **PET scan** is like pouring a fluorescent dye into the engine to find blockages. Each "tool" reveals different "faults." --- ## 1.3 Why Biomedical Imaging Matters — The "Clinical Value Chain" The clinical value of any imaging application can be reduced to **five core questions**: 1. **Is there a disease?** (Detection / Screening) 2. **What is the disease?** (Diagnosis / Characterization) 3. **How severe is it?** (Staging / Grading) 4. **Where exactly is it?** (Localization / Image-guided intervention) 5. **Is the treatment working?** (Therapy monitoring / Follow-up) Each modality excels at some of these questions and is weak at others — this is the **fundamental trade-off** that drives the choice of imaging application. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** A useful rule of thumb is the **diagnostic accuracy hierarchy** — Sensitivity ≥ 80%, Specificity ≥ 90% are considered clinically acceptable for screening applications. Spatial resolution below **1 mm** is required for most *structural* imaging of small lesions. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Biomedical Imaging Modalities Biomedical imaging systems are broadly classified along **two orthogonal axes**: | Axis | Class A | Class B | |---|---|---| | **Energy Used** | Ionizing (X-ray, CT, PET, SPECT) | Non-ionizing (MRI, Ultrasound, Optical) | | **Information Type** | Structural / Anatomical (X-ray, CT, MRI, US) | Functional / Molecular (PET, SPECT, fMRI) | This classification forms the foundation for selecting the right application for the right clinical scenario. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Imaging Application Selection Every imaging application decision rests on three engineering pillars: ### Pillar 1: **Physical Contrast Mechanism** Tissues must have **distinguishable physical properties** to generate image contrast. | Modality | Physical Property Exploited | |---|---| | X-ray / CT | Linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ (electron density, atomic number $Z$) | | MRI | Proton density $\rho$, relaxation times $T_1, T_2$, chemical shift | | Ultrasound | Acoustic impedance $Z = \rho c$ and its discontinuities | | PET / SPECT | Concentration of administered radiotracer (Bq/mL) | | Optical | Absorption coefficient $\mu_a$, scattering coefficient $\mu_s'$ | ### Pillar 2: **Spatial & Temporal Resolution** The application dictates whether we need to see **fine detail** or **rapid dynamics**. - **Spatial resolution** $d_{min}$ = smallest distinguishable object size. - **Temporal resolution** $\Delta t$ = minimum time between successive image frames. ### Pillar 3: **Safety & Accessibility** - **ALARA** principle governs radiation-based modalities. - **MRI** contraindications: pacemakers, ferromagnetic implants. - **Ultrasound** is the **only real-time, bedside, radiation-free modality**. --- ## 2.2 Detailed Applications by Modality ### 2.2.1 X-Ray Imaging Applications - **Radiography:** Bone fractures, pneumonia, dental caries, chest screening. - **Fluoroscopy:** Real-time guidance for catheterization, GI tract studies (barium swallow). - **Mammography:** Low-dose X-ray dedicated to breast cancer screening (requires $\leq 0.4$ mm resolution). - **Angiography:** Visualization of blood vessels using iodinated contrast agents. ### 2.2.2 Computed Tomography (CT) Applications - **Trauma imaging:** Rapid whole-body CT for accident victims. - **Oncology staging:** Tumor size, lymph node involvement, metastasis detection. - **Cardiology:** Coronary CT angiography (CCTA) for plaque visualization. - **Neurology:** Acute stroke differentiation (ischemic vs hemorrhagic within minutes). ### 2.2.3 MRI Applications - **Neuroimaging:** Brain tumors, multiple sclerosis (MS), stroke. - **Musculoskeletal:** Ligament tears (ACL), cartilage damage, spinal cord injury. - **Cardiac MRI:** Viability assessment, congenital heart disease. - **Functional MRI (fMRI):** Brain activation mapping via BOLD signal. - **MR Spectroscopy:** Non-invasive biochemical analysis of metabolites. ### 2.2.4 Ultrasound Applications - **Obstetrics:** Fetal growth monitoring, anomaly scans — safest modality for pregnancy. - **Cardiology (Echocardiography):** Valve function, ejection fraction. -
