$$W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)S_{ff}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 S_{ff}(u,v) + S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)} = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + \dfrac{S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}{S_{ff}(u,v)}}$$

where $S_{ff}$ and $S_{\eta\eta}$ are the power spectral densities of the signal and noise respectively.

**(b)** An $8$-bit grayscale image ($L=255$) of size $256 \times 256$ is restored from a noisy version. The sum of squared differences between the original and the restored image is $8 \times 10^6$. Calculate the MSE and PSNR. Comment on the quality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Compute MSE (2 marks):*

$$M \times N = 256 \times 256 = 65{,}536$$

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{65{,}536} = 122.07$$

*Step 2 — Compute PSNR (3 marks):*

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{L^2}{\text{MSE}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{255^2}{122.07}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{65{,}025}{122.07}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(532.71)$$

$$= 10 \times 2.7265 = 27.27 \text{ dB}$$

*Step 3 — Comment (2 marks):*

A PSNR of **27.27 dB** is acceptable but not excellent. Values above **30 dB** are typically visually pleasing; above **40 dB** indicate near-perfect reconstruction. Since 27.27 dB < 30 dB, the restoration is of moderate quality, and a stronger filter (e.g., BM3D or Wiener with better noise estimation) may be required.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the following spatial-domain filters with suitable equations: (i) Median filter, (ii) Harmonic mean filter, (iii) Contraharmonic mean filter. State the type of noise each filter is best suited for. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*(i) Median Filter (2.5 marks):*

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \text{median}\{g(s,t) \mid (s,t) \in S_{xy}\}$$

The median filter replaces the central pixel with the **median** of intensities in an $m \times n$ window. It is **nonlinear** and exceptionally good at removing **salt-and-pepper (impulse) noise** while preserving edges. Computation: sort the windowed pixels and pick the middle value.

*(ii) Harmonic Mean Filter (2 marks):*

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \frac{mn}{\sum_{(s,t) \in S_{xy}} \dfrac{1}{g(s,t)}}$$

The harmonic mean works well for **salt noise** (white pixels) and **Gaussian noise**, but fails for pepper noise (zero values cause division by zero).

*(iii) Contraharmonic Mean Filter (2.5 marks):*

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \frac{\sum g(s,t)^{Q+1}}{\sum g(s,t)^Q}$$

The order $Q$ controls filter behavior:
- $Q > 0$: removes **pepper** (black) noise.
- $Q < 0$: removes **salt** (white) noise.
- $Q = 0$: reduces to arithmetic mean.
- It cannot remove both salt AND pepper simultaneously; sign of $Q$ must match the noise.

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **homomorphic filtering** approach for removing multiplicative (speckle) noise in ultrasound images. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*Concept (2 marks):* Speckle noise in ultrasound and SAR imaging is **multiplicative**: $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \cdot \eta(x,y)$. Standard additive-noise filters (Wiener, Mean) fail because the noise variance scales with signal intensity.

*Block Diagram (3 marks):*

```
Image g(x,y)  →  Log transform ln g  →  FFT  →  Wiener filter W(u,v)
Wiener output  →  IFFT  →  Exponential exp(.)  →  Restored f̂(x,y)
```

*Step-by-step (2 marks):*

1. **Logarithmic transform** converts multiplicative noise to additive: $\ln g = \ln f + \ln \eta$.
2. Apply **Fourier transform**: $G_{log}(u,v) = F_{log}(u,v) + N_{log}(u,v)$.
3. Apply **Wiener filter** $W(u,v)$ in the frequency domain to suppress $N_{log}$.
4. Apply **inverse FFT** and then **exponential** to recover the image in the original domain.

This approach works because it transforms a difficult multiplicative-noise problem into a tractable additive-noise problem that the Wiener filter solves optimally in the MSE sense.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the conjugate $H^*$**: Many students write $W(u,v) = \frac{H(u,v)}{|H|^2 + S_\eta/S_f}$ instead of $\frac{H^*}{|H|^2 + S_\eta/S_f}$. The conjugate is **mandatory** because $H$ is complex-valued; the numerator must satisfy the Hermitian symmetry of the optimal filter. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Using pipes in LaTeX inside tables**: Causes markdown rendering failure. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 3. **Confusing MSE with RMSE**: RMSE is the square root of MSE; both must be in **bold units** in the answer.
> 4. **PSNR without specifying $L$**: Always state $L = 255$ for 8-bit images. Examiners award a separate mark for the correct substitution.
> 5. **Median filter incorrectly classified as linear**: It is a **rank-order nonlinear** filter. Common error; lose 1 mark.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Degradation Model**: Always begin with $g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ and its Fourier counterpart $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$.
- ✅ **Three restoration paradigms**: Inverse (unstable), Wiener (optimal linear MSE), Constrained Least Squares (regularized when $S_{ff}$ unknown).
- ✅ **Wiener Formula** (must memorize): $W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^*}{\vert H \vert^2 + S_{\eta\eta}/S_{ff}}$.
- ✅ **Filter Noise Map**: Median → impulse; Arithmetic mean → uniform; Wiener → Gaussian; Lee/Frost → speckle; Notch → periodic; NLM/BM3D → Rician (MRI).
- ✅ **MSE in numerator** of PSNR denominator; **PSNR in dB**; $L = 255$ for 8-bit; threshold for "good" quality ≈ **30 dB**.
- ✅ **Rician noise** dominates in MRI magnitude images (not Gaussian). Apply NLM or BM3D, not Wiener.
- ✅ **Speckle = multiplicative**; use homomorphic (log + Wiener + exp) pipeline.
- ✅ **Poisson noise**: Apply Anscombe variance-stabilizing transform first, then Wiener.
- ✅ **Convolution theorem** is the bridge between spatial-domain filtering and frequency-domain restoration.
- ✅ **Why Wiener beats inverse**: The term $S_{\eta\eta}/S_{ff}$ regularizes the denominator, preventing division by zero (instability) when $H \to 0$.
- ✅ **Adaptive filters** compute local statistics ($\sigma_L^2$, $\bar{g}_L$) within a window and modify the filter response per-pixel — better edge preservation than global filters.
- ✅ **KTU frequently tests**: PSNR numerical computation, identification of noise type from histogram, and derivation of the Wiener filter.

> **END OF NOTES — PEAET636 / Module 2 / Image Restoration and De-noising Techniques**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Watershed, Level Set, and Graph Cut
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET636 - BIOMEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Watershed, Level Set, and Graph Cut

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Watershed Transform

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **Watershed Transform** is a morphology-based, region-oriented image segmentation technique that interprets the 2D grayscale image as a 3D topographic surface, where pixel intensity represents elevation, and constructs "dam walls" (watershed lines) at the locations where water flowing from different regional minima would merge.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine you are standing on a hilly terrain made of sand. You pour water slowly from above. The water trickles down into the lowest valleys and forms separate pools. Now imagine continuing to raise the water level from below — the pools will rise. The moment two neighbouring pools are about to touch each other, you build a dam wall between them so they never merge. After the entire terrain is submerged, every pool corresponds to one segmented region, and every dam-wall corresponds to a boundary. The dams are exactly the **watershed lines**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Watershed is **not** a classical edge-detection method. It is a **region-growing** method whose output boundaries are guaranteed to be closed and continuous, which is critical in medical imaging (e.g., tumor boundary extraction).

---

### 1.2 Level Set Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **Level Set Method**, introduced by Osher and Sethian (1988), is an implicit, topology-adaptive numerical framework for tracking the evolution of a closed curve $\mathcal{C}(t)$ as the zero level set of a time-varying scalar function $\phi(\mathbf{x}, t) \in \mathbb{R}$ defined in one higher dimension.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Picture an elastic rubber band lying on a flat table, surrounding an object. Now slowly inflate the rubber band outward (or deflate it inward) so it tightly hugs the object — even if the object has a hole or splits into two pieces. The "rubber band" is the level set, but instead of tracking every point on the band (which is hard when it splits), we track a *surface* in 3D whose bottom contour (the curve touching the table) is always the rubber band. This way, splitting and merging happen automatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The level set method handles **topological changes** (splitting and merging) naturally without any explicit re-parametrisation, which classical "snakes" (Kass et al., 1988) cannot do.

---

### 1.3 Graph Cut Segmentation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Graph Cut** is a global, combinatorial optimisation technique that formulates image segmentation as a minimum s-t cut problem on a weighted graph, solved exactly by the **Max-Flow / Min-Cut Theorem** of Ford and Fulkerson (1956), and is widely used in interactive medical segmentation (Boykov–Kolmogorov, GrabCut).

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a chocolate bar made of small square cells. Some cells are chocolate, some are nuts (the object), and the rest is plain chocolate (the background). Every cell is a node. Now you want to *cut* the bar into two pieces such that the "cut" — the total length/weight of edges you slice — is as small as possible, and yet you must *guarantee* that a marked "source" piece stays connected to the source and a marked "sink" piece stays connected to the sink. The minimum-cost set of cut edges is exactly the optimum segmentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Graph cut produces a **globally optimal** solution (for the chosen energy function) in polynomial time, in contrast to watershed (local, greedy) or level set (local, PDE-based).

---

### 1.4 Comparison Snapshot

| Feature | Watershed | Level Set | Graph Cut |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mathematical basis | Mathematical morphology | PDE (Hamilton–Jacobi) | Combinatorial optimisation |
| Output | Closed boundaries (lines) | Closed contour | Pixel/object labels |
| Topology handling | Cannot split existing regions | Splits and merges freely | Binary label per pixel |
| Optimality | Local (greedy) | Local (PDE) | Global (max-flow) |
| Speed | Very fast | Moderate | Moderate |
| Typical biomedical use | Cell counting, lesion seed points | Tumor boundary refinement | Interactive tumor, organ delineation |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Watershed Transform — Operational Theory

**Topographic View:**
Let $I(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}$ be a grayscale image. Treat $I$ as the elevation function $z = I(x,y)$. The **Catchment Basin** $\mathcal{B}(m_i)$ of a regional minimum $m_i$ is the set of all pixels whose steepest-descent path terminates at $m_i$. The **watershed** is the set of points belonging to no unique catchment basin.

**Two Principal Algorithms:**

1. **Immersion (Vincent–Soille, 1991):** Simulate flooding the topography with water rising from below through holes pierced at each regional minimum. Two-basin merges are detected using a **Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)** data structure.
2. **Rainfall (Meyer, 1994):** A drop of water is released at every pixel; it follows the path of steepest descent until it reaches a minimum. All pixels routed to the same minimum belong to one catchment basin.

**Over-Segmentation Problem:**
Direct application produces thousands of tiny regions because of noise and texture-induced spurious minima. KTU-canonical fix: **Marker-Controlled Watershed**.

Let $\mathcal{M}_{\text{in}}$ = set of *internal markers* (definitely inside the object) and $\mathcal{M}_{\text{out}}$ = set of *external markers* (definitely background). Compute the **minima imposition**:

$$
I_{\text{marked}}(x,y) = 
\begin{cases}
0, & (x,y) \in \mathcal{M}_{\text{in}} \cup \mathcal{M}_{\text{out}} \\
I(x,y) + \epsilon, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Apply watershed only on $I_{\text{marked}}$ — flooding is forced to start exactly at user seeds.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard preprocessing pipeline (KTU favourite):**
> $I \xrightarrow{\text{gradient}} \vert\nabla I \xrightarrow{\text{h-minima}} \text{suppress shallow minima} \xrightarrow{\text{watershed}} \text{clean segmentation}$

---

### 2.2 Level Set Method — Operational Theory

Let $\mathcal{C}(t)$ be a closed evolving curve. Define the **level set function**:

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x}, t) : \mathbb{R}^{2} \times \mathbb{R}^{+} \to \mathbb{R}, \quad \mathcal{C}(t) = \{ \mathbf{x} : \phi(\mathbf{x}, t) = 0 \}
$$

With the convention $\phi < 0$ **inside** and $\phi > 0$ **outside**.

The **Curve Evolution PDE** is:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial t} = F \mathbf{N}
$$

where $F$ is the **speed function** and $\mathbf{N}$ is the outward unit normal. Substituting $\mathcal{C}(t) = \{ \phi = 0 \}$ gives the **Level Set Equation**:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + F \vert \nabla \phi \vert = 0\;}
$$

**Speed Function Components (general image-based form):**

$$
F = \underbrace{\alpha}_{\text{balloon}} + \underbrace{\beta \kappa}_{\text{curvature (smoothness)}} + \underbrace{F_{\text{image}}}_{\text{data attachment}}
$$

where $\kappa = \text{div}\!\left(\frac{\nabla \phi}{\vert\nabla\phi\vert}\right)$ is the mean curvature of the level set.

**Chan–Vese Region-Based Speed (2001):**

$$
F = -\lambda_{1}\bigl(I - c_{1}\bigr)^{2} + \lambda_{2}\bigl(I - c_{2}\bigr)^{2} + \mu\,\kappa - \nu
$$

where $c_{1}, c_{2}$ are the mean intensities inside and outside the curve.

**Re-initialisation (Sussman, 1994):**
After several iterations, $\phi$ loses the signed-distance property. Reset it to a true signed-distance function:

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \text{sign}(\phi_{0})\,(1 - \vert\nabla\phi\vert)
$$

Run this as a separate PDE until steady state.

---

### 2.3 Graph Cut — Operational Theory

Construct a graph $G = (V, E)$ with:
- Two special **terminal** nodes: source $s$ (object) and sink $t$ (background).
- One node per pixel $p \in \mathcal{P}$, plus the two terminals.
- Edges:
  - **t-links** $(p, s)$ and $(p, t)$ carrying data penalties $D_p(\text{obj})$ and $D_p(\text{bg})$.
  - **n-links** $(p, q)$ for neighbouring pixels, carrying smoothness penalties $V_{pq}$.

A cut $\mathcal{C} \subset E$ partitions $V$ into $S$ (containing $s$) and $T$ (containing $t$). The cut cost:

$$
\vert \mathcal{C} \vert = \sum_{(u,v) \in \mathcal{C}} w(u,v)
$$

**Energy to minimise:**

$$
\boxed{\;E(L) = \sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} D_p(L_p) \;+\; \sum_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{N}} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)\;}
$$

where $L_p \in \{\text{obj}, \text{bg}\}$ is the label of pixel $p$.

**Max-Flow / Min-Cut Theorem (Ford–Fulkerson):**

$$
\boxed{\;\max_{s \to t} \text{flow} \;=\; \min_{\mathcal{C}} \vert \mathcal{C} \vert\;}
$$

Hence minimising cut cost = maximising flow from $s$ to $t$ — solvable exactly in $O\bigl(\vert V\vert^{2} \cdot \vert E\vert^{1/2}\bigr)$ using Boykov–Kolmogorov (2004) for vision graphs.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nabla I = \bigl(\partial I/\partial x,\ \partial I/\partial y\bigr)$ | Image gradient | Watershed preprocessing |
| 2 | $\vert \nabla I \vert = \sqrt{(\partial_x I)^{2} + (\partial_y I)^{2}}$ | Gradient magnitude | Watershed input |
| 3 | $\kappa = \text{div}\!\bigl(\nabla\phi / \vert \nabla\phi \vert\bigr)$ | Mean curvature | Level set smoothing |
| 4 | $\partial_t \phi + F \vert \nabla\phi \vert = 0$ | Level set PDE | Level set evolution |
| 5 | $F = -\lambda_{1}(I - c_{1})^{2} + \lambda_{2}(I - c_{2})^{2} + \mu \kappa - \nu$ | Chan–Vese speed | Region-based active contour |
| 6 | $\partial_t \phi = \text{sign}(\phi_0)(1 - \vert\nabla\phi\vert)$ | Re-initialisation PDE | Level set maintenance |
| 7 | $E(L) = \sum D_p(L_p) + \sum V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$ | Graph cut energy | Segmentation |
| 8 | $D_p(\text{obj}) = -\ln \text{Pr}(I_p \mid \text{obj})$ | Negative log-likelihood t-link | Graph cut data term |
| 9 | $V_{pq} = \gamma \exp\!\bigl(-\vert I_p - I_q \vert^{2} / (2\sigma^{2})\bigr) \cdot \frac{1}{\text{dist}(p,q)}$ | N-link weight (Potts) | Graph cut smoothness |
| 10 | $\max \text{ flow}(s,t) = \min \vert \mathcal{C} \vert$ | Max-Flow / Min-Cut theorem | Graph cut optimality |
| 11 | $C(t) = \{\mathbf{x} : \phi(\mathbf{x},t) = 0\}$ | Implicit curve definition | Level set |
| 12 | $H(\phi) = \mathbf{1}[\phi \ge 0]$ | Heaviside step | Chan–Vese update of $c_1, c_2$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Watershed is used in *hematology cell counters* (blood smear), level set in *cardiac MRI wall tracking*, and graph cut in *liver tumor delineation* (used in Siemens/Philips clinical workstations).

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Level Set PDE from Curve Evolution

**Step 1 — Start with the implicit surface definition.**
At every instant $t$, the curve $\mathcal{C}(t)$ is exactly the zero level set:

$$
\phi\bigl(\mathbf{x}(t), t\bigr) = 0, \quad \forall \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$ (chain rule).**
Since $\phi$ is constant (= 0) along the moving curve:

$$
\frac{d\phi}{dt} = 0
$$

Expanding using the multivariable chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \sum_{i=1}^{2} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_i} \frac{d x_i}{dt} = 0
$$

In vector notation:

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \nabla \phi \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the velocity of the curve.**
The curve moves with velocity $V = d\mathbf{x}/dt$ in the direction of the outward unit normal:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = V \mathbf{N}
$$

where $\mathbf{N} = -\dfrac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert}$ (negative because $\phi$ decreases outward when $\phi<0$ inside).

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \nabla \phi \cdot (V \mathbf{N}) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + V \bigl(\nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf{N}\bigr) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the normal vector expression.**

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} - V \nabla\phi \cdot \frac{\nabla\phi}{\vert\nabla\phi\vert} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} - V \frac{\vert\nabla\phi\vert^{2}}{\vert\nabla\phi\vert} = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the canonical level set equation.**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + V \vert \nabla\phi \vert = 0\;}
$$

This matches Section 2.2 with $F = V$, completing the derivation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mean Curvature Term

We need $\kappa = \text{div}\bigl(\nabla\phi / \vert\nabla\phi\vert\bigr)$ in 2D. Write $\mathbf{n} = \nabla\phi / \vert\nabla\phi\vert = (n_x, n_y)$.

$$
\text{div}\,\mathbf{n} = \frac{\partial n_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial n_y}{\partial y}
$$

Compute $\partial n_x / \partial x$:

$$
n_x = \frac{\phi_x}{\sqrt{\phi_x^{2} + \phi_y^{2}}}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial n_x}{\partial x} = \frac{\phi_{xx} \sqrt{\phi_x^{2} + \phi_y^{2}} - \phi_x \cdot \dfrac{\phi_x \phi_{xx} + \phi_y \phi_{xy}}{\sqrt{\phi_x^{2} + \phi_y^{2}}}}{\phi_x^{2} + \phi_y^{2}}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\frac{\partial n_x}{\partial x} = \frac{\phi_{xx}(\phi_x^{2} + \phi_y^{2}) - \phi_x(\phi_x \phi_{xx} + \phi_y \phi_{xy})}{(\phi_x^{2} + \phi_y^{2})^{3/2}}
$$

Similarly for $\partial n_y / \partial y$. Adding:

$$
\kappa = \frac{\phi_{xx}\phi_y^{2} - 2\phi_x\phi_y\phi_{xy} + \phi_{yy}\phi_x^{2}}{(\phi_x^{2} + \phi_y^{2})^{3/2}}
$$

This is the curvature scalar used in the level set equation's smoothing term.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Watershed via Region Growing

**Step 1 — Define geodesic influence.**
For every pixel $p$, define the **cost of reaching minimum $m_i$**:

$$
d(p, m_i) = \min_{\pi : p \to m_i} \int_{\pi} \vert \nabla I(\mathbf{x}) \vert \, ds
$$

where $\pi$ is any path from $p$ to $m_i$.

**Step 2 — Catchment basin.**

$$
\mathcal{B}(m_i) = \{ p : d(p, m_i) < d(p, m_j)\ \forall j \ne i \}
$$

**Step 3 — Watershed set.**

$$
W = \bigcap_{i \ne j} \bigl( d(\cdot, m_i) = d(\cdot, m_j) \bigr)
$$

This is the set of points equidistant (in gradient-weighted path length) from two minima — the natural dam location.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Max-Flow / Min-Cut Equivalence (Sketch)

**Step 1 — Flow / Cut definitions.**
- A flow $f : E \to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ satisfies: (i) capacity $f(e) \le c(e)$, (ii) flow conservation at non-terminal nodes.
- An s-t cut $\mathcal{C} \subset E$ partitions $V$ into $S \ni s$ and $T \ni t$. Capacity $\vert\mathcal{C}\vert = \sum_{e \in \mathcal{C}} c(e)$.

**Step 2 — Trivial inequality.**
Every s-t cut must be crossed by every s-t flow. Hence:

$$
\vert f \vert \le \vert \mathcal{C} \vert \quad \forall f, \mathcal{C}
$$

Taking maximum over $f$ and minimum over $\mathcal{C}$:

$$
\max \vert f \vert \le \min \vert \mathcal{C} \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Augmenting-path construction.**
Define the **residual graph** $G_f$ with edges having remaining capacity. If no augmenting $s \to t$ path exists, define $S = \{u \text{ reachable from } s\}$ and $T = V \setminus S$. The cut $\mathcal{C}$ of edges from $S$ to $T$ satisfies $f(e) = c(e)$ for all $e \in \mathcal{C}$ (saturated). Hence:

$$
\vert \mathcal{C} \vert = \sum_{e \in \mathcal{C}} f(e) = \vert f \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

$$
\max \vert f \vert = \min \vert \mathcal{C} \vert \qquad \blacksquare
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This theorem guarantees that solving an $s \to t$ flow problem automatically solves the segmentation optimisation, making graph cut an *exact* (globally optimal) method.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Graph Cut on 1-D Toy Image)

Let three pixels have intensities $\{80, 120, 90\}$ (where 120 is foreground, others background).

**Step 1 — t-links (data term).**
Foreground mean = 120, Background mean = 85.

$$
D_p(\text{obj}) = (I_p - 120)^{2}, \quad D_p(\text{bg}) = (I_p - 85)^{2}
$$

| Pixel $p$ | $I_p$ | $D_p(\text{obj})$ | $D_p(\text{bg})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80 | 1600 | 25 |
| 2 | 120 | 0 | 1225 |
| 3 | 90 | 900 | 25 |

**Step 2 — n-links (smoothness).** Use Potts model with $\gamma = 100$:
$\vert I_1 - I_2\vert = 40 \Rightarrow V_{12} = 100\exp(-40^{2}/200) \approx 0$
$\vert I_2 - I_3\vert = 30 \Rightarrow V_{23} = 100\exp(-30^{2}/200) \approx 11$

**Step 3 — Minimum cut.**
Label $p_2$ as **object** (cost = 0 to source), $p_1, p_3$ as **background** (cost 25 each to sink). Total data cost = $0 + 25 + 25 = 50$. Smoothness cost = $0 + 11 = 11$ (no penalty since $p_2$ and $p_3$ differ in label but intensity difference is large → small penalty already accounted).

**Optimal cut cost** $= 50 + 11 = \mathbf{61}$.

This is the global optimum — no other label assignment gives lower energy.

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
Define the **Watershed Transform**. Why is it preferred over classical edge detection for medical image segmentation?

**Model Answer:**
The watershed transform interprets the grayscale image $I(x,y)$ as a topographic surface where intensity equals elevation. It floods this surface from regional minima and constructs "dam walls" (watershed lines) wherever two catchment basins are about to merge. The output is the set of catchment basin labels plus the watershed boundaries. It is preferred over edge detectors (Sobel, Canny) because:
1. It always produces **closed, continuous contours**, avoiding broken edges common with gradient methods.
2. It is a **region-based** approach, robust to weak edges.
3. It directly gives labelled regions, not just boundaries.

*(Expected length: 8–10 lines. Marks awarded for: definition 1, topographic analogy 1, three advantages 1.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
What is a **level set function**? State the level set evolution PDE.

**Model Answer:**
A level set function $\phi(\mathbf{x}, t)$ is a time-varying scalar field in one higher dimension whose **zero level set** at every instant defines the evolving curve $\mathcal{C}(t) = \{\mathbf{x} : \phi(\mathbf{x}, t) = 0\}$. Conventionally $\phi < 0$ inside the curve and $\phi > 0$ outside.

The evolution PDE is:

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + F \vert \nabla \phi \vert = 0
$$

where $F$ is the speed function (e.g., $F = -\lambda_1(I - c_1)^2 + \lambda_2(I - c_2)^2 + \mu \kappa - \nu$ for Chan–Vese). The function's curvature term $\kappa$ ensures smoothness while image-based terms drive the contour toward object boundaries. *(Expected: definition 1, zero-level-set concept 1, equation with all symbols 1.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Watershed Focused** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Immersion Algorithm** of watershed segmentation. Discuss the **over-segmentation problem** and how **marker-controlled watershed** resolves it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Immersion Algorithm (Vincent–Soille, 1991):** The grayscale image is treated as a topographic surface. Holes are pierced at every regional minimum. The terrain is then slowly immersed into water. As water rises, catchment basins fill with water; when water from two basins is about to merge, a 1-pixel-wide dam is constructed. After full immersion, every basin is labelled and every dam is the watershed line.

2. **Algorithm Steps:**
   - Sort all pixels by intensity (O(N log N)).
   - Process pixels in increasing intensity order using a **priority queue**.
   - Maintain a queue of *currently reachable* pixels.
   - Use **Union-Find** to label connected components of already-processed pixels.
   - When a newly processed pixel touches a pixel of a different label, mark it as a watershed pixel.

3. **Over-segmentation:** Caused by spurious minima from noise, texture, and quantisation. Produces hundreds of meaningless regions for a single organ.

4. **Marker-Controlled Fix:**
   - Compute **internal markers** $\mathcal{M}_{\text{in}}$ via morphological opening-by-reconstruction (objects definitely inside).
   - Compute **external markers** $\mathcal{M}_{\text{out}}$ via watershed of the distance transform of internal markers (background definitely outside).
   - Impose minima: replace $I(x,y)$ with a value strictly greater than any marker.
   - Apply watershed on this modified surface → flooding starts exactly at the seeds, eliminating spurious basins.

*(Marks: Immersion diagram 2, steps 2, over-seg explanation 1, marker solution 2.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the **Level Set Method** for curve evolution. Derive the level set PDE from the curve evolution equation $\partial_t \mathcal{C} = F \mathbf{N}$. Mention two advantages over parametric snakes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Concept:** Represent an evolving curve $\mathcal{C}(t)$ implicitly as the zero level set of $\phi(\mathbf{x}, t)$. Define $\mathcal{C}(t) = \{\mathbf{x} : \phi(\mathbf{x}, t) = 0\}$ with $\phi < 0$ inside.

2. **Derivation (as in Section 3.1):**
   - Differentiate $\phi(\mathbf{x}(t), t) = 0$: $\partial_t \phi + \nabla \phi \cdot d\mathbf{x}/dt = 0$.
   - Substitute $d\mathbf{x}/dt = V \mathbf{N}$ and $\mathbf{N} = -\nabla\phi/\vert\nabla\phi\vert$.
   - Obtain $\partial_t \phi + V \vert \nabla \phi \vert = 0$.

3. **Speed Function Choices:**
   - Edge-based: $F = \frac{1}{1 + \vert\nabla G_\sigma * I\vert^{2}}$ — slow near edges.
   - Region-based (Chan–Vese): $F = -\lambda_1(I - c_1)^2 + \lambda_2(I - c_2)^2 + \mu \kappa - \nu$ — robust to weak edges.

4. **Advantages over Snakes:**
   - **Topological flexibility:** Naturally handles splitting/merging without re-parametrisation.
   - **Intrinsic geometry:** Works directly in image grid; no need for control points.
   - **Numerically stable** via upwind finite differences and re-initialisation.

*(Marks: implicit representation 2, derivation 3, advantages 2.)*

---

#### **Question B — Graph Cut Focused** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Formulate **image segmentation as a graph cut problem**. Draw the s-t network and state the Max-Flow / Min-Cut theorem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Graph Construction:**
   - Nodes: every pixel $p \in \mathcal{P}$ plus source $s$ and sink $t$.
   - Edges: t-links $(p, s)$ and $(p, t)$ carrying data costs $D_p(\text{obj})$ and $D_p(\text{bg})$; n-links $(p, q)$ for 4- or 8-neighbour pixels carrying smoothness $V_{pq}$.

2. **Energy Functional:**

$$
E(L) = \sum_{p} D_p(L_p) + \sum_{(p,q)} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)
$$

3. **Network Sketch:** (Refer to Diagram 4.3 above for the topology.)

4. **Max-Flow / Min-Cut Theorem (Ford–Fulkerson, 1956):**

$$
\max \text{flow}(s \to t) = \min \vert \mathcal{C} \vert
$$

Hence minimising the cut cost (segmentation energy) equals finding the maximum flow, solvable exactly.

*(Marks: graph construction 2, energy 2, theorem statement 2, diagram 1.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the **Chan–Vese Level Set Model** for region-based segmentation. State its evolution equation and discuss why it is robust to weak edges. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Idea:** Chan–Vese (2001) treats segmentation as finding a contour $\mathcal{C}$ that minimises the Mumford–Shah functional:

$$
F(c_1, c_2, \mathcal{C}) = \mu \cdot \text{Length}(\mathcal{C}) + \nu \cdot \text{Area}(\text{inside } \mathcal{C}) + \lambda_1 \int_{\text{inside}} (I - c_1)^2 \, dA + \lambda_2 \int_{\text{outside}} (I - c_2)^2 \, dA
$$

where $c_1, c_2$ are mean intensities inside and outside.

2. **Level Set Formulation (using Heaviside $H$ and Dirac $\delta$):**

$$
F_\varepsilon(\phi, c_1, c_2) = \mu \int \delta_\varepsilon(\phi) \vert \nabla \phi \vert \, dA + \nu \int H_\varepsilon(\phi) \, dA + \lambda_1 \int (I - c_1)^2 H_\varepsilon(\phi) \, dA + \lambda_2 \int (I - c_2)^2 (1 - H_\varepsilon(\phi)) \, dA
$$

3. **Evolution Equation (Euler–Lagrange):**

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \delta_\varepsilon(\phi)\bigl[\mu \, \text{div}(\nabla\phi / \vert\nabla\phi\vert) - \nu - \lambda_1 (I - c_1)^2 + \lambda_2 (I - c_2)^2\bigr]
$$

with $c_1 = \dfrac{\int I H_\varepsilon(\phi)}{\int H_\varepsilon(\phi)}$ and $c_2 = \dfrac{\int I (1 - H_\varepsilon(\phi))}{\int (1 - H_\varepsilon(\phi))}$.

4. **Robustness to weak edges:** Because the model uses **region statistics** ($c_1, c_2$) and not just the image gradient, the contour is pulled toward the true boundary even when edges are blurred, low-contrast, or noisy — critical for medical ultrasound and MRI.

*(Marks: functional 2, level set form 2, evolution equation 2, robustness 1.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $D_p(\text{obj})$ and $V_{pq}$.** Students routinely mix up t-links (data) and n-links (smoothness) and lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Sign convention matters.** In level set, $\phi < 0$ inside. Forgetting this flips the entire evolution direction.
> 3. **Watershed ≠ edge detection.** Examiners deduct marks for writing "watershed detects edges" — it is a **region-labeling** method whose *boundaries* emerge from region labelling.
> 4. **Curvature $\kappa$ sign:** Always include $\mu > 0$ as the smoothness coefficient; writing $\mu < 0$ makes the contour blow up.
> 5. **In graph cut, Min-Cut = Min $\vert\mathcal{C}\vert$, NOT Max-Flow.** Statement must be exact; examiners check the equality sign and theorem attribution (Ford–Fulkerson).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ☐ Watershed = topographic flooding; output = **closed boundaries + labelled regions**.
- ☐ Over-segmentation fix = **marker-controlled watershed** using morphological opening-by-reconstruction.
- ☐ Level set = implicit curve as **zero level set** of $\phi$; handles topology changes.
- ☐ Key PDE: $\partial_t \phi + F \vert \nabla\phi\vert = 0$.
- ☐ Chan–Vese speed: $F = -\lambda_1(I - c_1)^2 + \lambda_2(I - c_2)^2 + \mu\kappa - \nu$.
- ☐ Re-initialisation PDE: $\partial_t \phi = \text{sign}(\phi_0)(1 - \vert\nabla\phi\vert)$.
- ☐ Graph cut = s-t graph with **t-links** (data) and **n-links** (smoothness).
- ☐ Energy: $E(L) = \sum D_p(L_p) + \sum V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$.
- ☐ Max-Flow = Min-Cut (Ford–Fulkerson) → globally optimal segmentation.
- ☐ Watershed = local/greedy; Level set = local/PDE; Graph cut = global/exact.
- ☐ Biomedical uses: cell counting (watershed), tumor boundary refinement (level set), interactive organ delineation (graph cut / GrabCut).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feature Extraction: Shape, Texture, and Intensity-based Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET636 - BIOMEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feature Extraction: Shape, Texture, and Intensity-based Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feature Extraction** is the process of transforming raw biomedical image data (pixels/voxels) into a compact, descriptive, and discriminative numerical representation called a **feature vector**. These features quantify specific image characteristics that are clinically or diagnostically meaningful, enabling subsequent tasks such as classification, segmentation validation, content-based image retrieval (CBIR), and computer-aided diagnosis (CAD).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEAET636 – Biomedical Imaging**, feature extraction is formally categorized into three principal domains:

| Feature Domain | What It Describes | Typical Clinical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape Features** | Geometric & morphological properties of a Region of Interest (ROI) | Tumor boundary irregularity, cell nucleus roundness |
| **Texture Features** | Spatial arrangement & variation of pixel intensities | Tissue differentiation (benign vs. malignant), tumor heterogeneity |
| **Intensity Features** | Statistical distribution of pixel gray-level values | Tissue density, contrast enhancement, perfusion analysis |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Detective at a Crime Scene":**
> Imagine you are a detective handed a photograph of a suspect. Before running a database search, you don't show the *entire photo* to the computer. Instead, you describe the suspect using measurable descriptors:
> - **"How tall is he?"** → **Shape feature** (e.g., perimeter, area, compactness)
> - **"Is his shirt plain, striped, or dotted?"** → **Texture feature** (e.g., contrast, entropy, homogeneity)
> - **"Is the photo bright, dark, or mid-gray overall?"** → **Intensity feature** (e.g., mean, variance, skewness)
>
> Each descriptor is a *feature*. A combination of them becomes a **feature vector** that uniquely fingerprints the image. In biomedical imaging, the "suspect" is a lesion, cell, or tissue, and the "fingerprint" drives diagnosis.

---

### 1.3 Physical & Mathematical Constants / Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard quantitative metrics are universally used in KTU-evaluated biomedical feature extraction problems:
> - **Image dimensions (for an M × N image):** **M** rows, **N** columns
> - **Gray levels:** **L** (typically $L = 256$ for 8-bit images, so $G = \{0, 1, ..., 255\}$)
> - **Number of pixels in ROI:** **N_p** (or **A** for Area in shape features)
> - **Spatial coordinates:** Pixel at position $(i, j)$ where $i$ is the row index and $j$ is the column index
> - **Histogram normalization factor:** $\frac{1}{N_p}$ where $N_p$ is the total number of pixels in the ROI

---

### 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping & Bloom's Taxonomy

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 CO Alignment:** This topic primarily satisfies **CO3** – *Apply feature extraction techniques to biomedical images for diagnostic interpretation.*
> **Bloom's Level:** Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4) → Evaluate (L5)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Shape-Based Features (Geometric / Morphological Descriptors)

Shape features describe the **geometric properties** of a segmented ROI (Region of Interest). They are computed *after* segmentation, treating the ROI as a binary mask.

#### 2.1.1 Area (A)
The number of pixels contained within the ROI boundary.

$$A = \sum_{(i,j) \in ROI} 1$$

#### 2.1.2 Perimeter (P)
The length of the outer boundary of the ROI, computed by counting boundary pixels.

#### 2.1.3 Compactness / Circularity (C)
A dimensionless ratio measuring how close a shape is to a perfect circle. A circle has $C = 1$; irregular shapes have $C < 1$.

$$C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{2}}$$

#### 2.1.4 Eccentricity (E)
Ratio of the major axis length to the minor axis length of the fitted ellipse.

$$E = \frac{\text{major axis length}}{\text{minor axis length}}$$

#### 2.1.5 Solidity (S)
Ratio of the ROI area to the area of its convex hull.

$$S = \frac{A}{A_{\text{convex hull}}}$$

#### 2.1.6 Moments (Statistical Shape Descriptors)
The raw 2-D moment of order $(p + q)$ is defined as:

$$M_{pq} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} i^{p}\, j^{q}\, I(i,j)$$

Central moments (translation-invariant):

$$\mu_{pq} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} (i - \bar{i})^{p}\,(j - \bar{j})^{q}\, I(i,j)$$

where $(\bar{i}, \bar{j})$ is the centroid.

---

### 2.2 Intensity-Based Features (First-Order Statistics)

These features are derived directly from the **histogram** of pixel gray-level values inside the ROI. They ignore spatial relationships.

Let $P(g)$ be the normalized histogram (probability of gray level $g$):

$$P(g) = \frac{n(g)}{N_p}, \quad g \in \{0, 1, ..., L-1\}$$

| Feature | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean (μ)** | $\mu = \sum_{g=0}^{L-1} g \cdot P(g)$ | Average brightness |
| **Variance (σ²)** | $\sigma^{2} = \sum_{g=0}^{L-1} (g - \mu)^{2}\, P(g)$ | Spread of intensities |
| **Standard Deviation (σ)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}}$ | Contrast magnitude |
| **Skewness (γ₁)** | $\gamma_{1} = \frac{1}{\sigma^{3}} \sum (g-\mu)^{3} P(g)$ | Asymmetry of histogram |
| **Kurtosis (γ₂)** | $\gamma_{2} = \frac{1}{\sigma^{4}} \sum (g-\mu)^{4} P(g) - 3$ | "Peakedness" of histogram |
| **Energy** | $E = \sum_{g} [P(g)]^{2}$ | Uniformity measure |
| **Entropy** | $H = -\sum_{g} P(g) \log_{2} P(g)$ | Randomness / information content |

---

### 2.3 Texture Features (Second-Order Statistics – Haralick Features)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Texture features capture the **spatial dependence** between neighboring pixels using the **Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)**, introduced by **Haralick et al. (1973)**.

#### 2.3.1 GLCM Definition
$G_{\Delta}(i, j)$ counts how often a pixel of intensity $i$ is adjacent (at offset $\Delta = (\Delta x, \Delta y)$) to a pixel of intensity $j$. Normalized GLCM:

$$P_{\Delta}(i,j) = \frac{G_{\Delta}(i,j)}{\sum_{i}\sum_{j} G_{\Delta}(i,j)}$$

#### 2.3.2 Haralick Descriptors (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet below uses `\vert` (not `|`) to prevent markdown table breakage — per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

| Feature | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Contrast** | $\sum_{i}\sum_{j} (i-j)^{2}\, P(i,j)$ | Local intensity variation |
| **Homogeneity (IDM)** | $\sum_{i}\sum_{j} \frac{P(i,j)}{1 + (i-j)^{2}}$ | Closeness to diagonal |
| **Energy (Angular 2nd Moment)** | $\sum_{i}\sum_{j} [P(i,j)]^{2}$ | Orderliness / uniformity |
| **Correlation** | $\sum_{i}\sum_{j} \frac{(i-\mu_{i})(j-\mu_{j})\, P(i,j)}{\sigma_{i}\, \sigma_{j}}$ | Linear dependency of gray levels |
| **Entropy** | $-\sum_{i}\sum_{j} P(i,j) \log P(i,j)$ | Texture complexity / randomness |
| **Dissimilarity** | $\sum_{i}\sum_{j} \vert i - j \vert\, P(i,j)$ | Weighted absolute difference |
| **Maximum Probability** | $\max_{i,j} P(i,j)$ | Strongest co-occurrence |

#### 2.3.3 Local Binary Pattern (LBP) – A Simpler Texture Operator

For each pixel, compare it with its 8 neighbors on a 3×3 patch:

$$LBP(x_c, y_c) = \sum_{p=0}^{7} s(g_p - g_c)\, 2^{p}$$

where $s(\cdot)$ is the sign function:

$$s(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & x \geq 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

---

### 2.4 Why and How – Structured Logic Steps

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are these three feature families needed?**
> 1. **Shape** answers: *"What does the lesion look like?"* → Geometric fingerprint
> 2. **Intensity** answers: *"How bright or dark is the tissue?"* → Density fingerprint
> 3. **Texture** answers: *"Is the tissue smooth, grainy, or heterogeneous?"* → Pattern fingerprint
>
> In CAD systems, all three are concatenated into a single **hybrid feature vector** $\mathbf{F} = [f_{\text{shape}}; f_{\text{intensity}}; f_{\text{texture}}]$ and fed to a classifier (SVM, KNN, Neural Net).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mammography CAD:** Texture + Shape features distinguish malignant masses (spiculated, heterogeneous) from benign ones (smooth, homogeneous).
- **Histopathology:** Shape features quantify nuclear pleomorphism (cancer grading).
- **MRI Brain Tumor Analysis:** Intensity features assess contrast enhancement; texture features grade glioma aggressiveness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Computing Shape Features (Area, Perimeter, Compactness)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A binary segmented ROI of a cell nucleus in a histopathology image has **Area $A = 200$ pixels** and **Perimeter $P = 60$ pixels**. Calculate the **Compactness (Circularity)**.

**Step 1:** Recall the compactness formula from Section 2.1.3.

$$C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $A = 200$ and $P = 60$.

$$C = \frac{4 \times \pi \times 200}{60^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator.

$$C = \frac{800\pi}{3600}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the fraction.

$$C = \frac{800}{3600}\, \pi = \frac{2}{9}\, \pi$$

**Step 5:** Numerical evaluation.

$$C = 0.2222 \times 3.1416 \approx 0.698$$

**Interpretation:** A compactness of **0.698** is well below 1, indicating the nucleus is **irregular** (not perfectly round) — a possible indicator of malignancy.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Intensity (First-Order) Feature Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 4×4 ROI patch has gray-level histogram (un-normalized) as: $n(0)=2,\ n(1)=4,\ n(2)=6,\ n(3)=4$. Compute **Mean (μ)**, **Variance (σ²)**, and **Entropy (H)**.

**Step 1:** Compute the total number of pixels $N_p$.

$$N_p = 2 + 4 + 6 + 4 = 16$$

**Step 2:** Normalize the histogram to obtain probabilities $P(g)$.

$$P(0) = \frac{2}{16} = 0.125,\quad P(1) = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25,\quad P(2) = \frac{6}{16} = 0.375,\quad P(3) = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25$$

**Step 3:** Compute the mean.

$$\mu = \sum_{g=0}^{3} g \cdot P(g) = (0)(0.125) + (1)(0.25) + (2)(0.375) + (3)(0.25)$$

$$\mu = 0 + 0.25 + 0.75 + 0.75 = 1.75$$

**Step 4:** Compute the variance.

$$\sigma^{2} = \sum_{g=0}^{3} (g - \mu)^{2}\, P(g)$$

**Step 4a:** Compute individual terms.

$$\begin{aligned} (0 - 1.75)^{2} \times 0.125 &= (3.0625)(0.125) = 0.3828 \\ (1 - 1.75)^{2} \times 0.25 &= (0.5625)(0.25) = 0.1406 \\ (2 - 1.75)^{2} \times 0.375 &= (0.0625)(0.375) = 0.0234 \\ (3 - 1.75)^{2} \times 0.25 &= (1.5625)(0.25) = 0.3906 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4b:** Sum the terms.

$$\sigma^{2} = 0.3828 + 0.1406 + 0.0234 + 0.3906 = 0.9374$$

**Step 5:** Compute the entropy in bits.

$$H = -\sum_{g=0}^{3} P(g)\, \log_{2} P(g)$$

**Step 5a:** Compute each logarithmic term.

$$\begin{aligned} -0.125 \log_{2}(0.125) &= -0.125 \times (-3) = 0.3750 \\ -0.25 \log_{2}(0.25) &= -0.25 \times (-2) = 0.5000 \\ -0.375 \log_{2}(0.375) &= -0.375 \times (-1.4150) = 0.5306 \\ -0.25 \log_{2}(0.25) &= 0.5000 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5b:** Sum the terms.

$$H = 0.3750 + 0.5000 + 0.5306 + 0.5000 = 1.9056 \text{ bits}$$

**Interpretation:** Mean ≈ 1.75 (mid-gray), variance ≈ 0.94 (moderate spread), entropy ≈ 1.91 bits (moderate randomness).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: GLCM Construction & Texture Feature

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 4×4 ROI has 4 gray levels $\{0,1,2,3\}$. Compute the **GLCM at offset $\Delta = (0,1)$ (horizontal neighbors)** and then find **Contrast** and **Homogeneity**.

**Step 1:** The image matrix $I$ is:

$$I = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 & 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 2 & 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Count horizontal neighbor pairs $(g_{\text{left}}, g_{\text{right}})$.

**Row 1:** $(0,0), (0,1), (1,1)$ → +1 to $G(0,0)$, +1 to $G(0,1)$, +1 to $G(1,1)$
**Row 2:** $(0,0), (0,1), (1,1)$ → same as above
**Row 3:** $(2,2), (2,3), (3,3)$ → +1 to $G(2,2)$, +1 to $G(2,3)$, +1 to $G(3,3)$
**Row 4:** $(2,2), (2,3), (3,3)$ → same as Row 3

**Step 3:** Build the GLCM (symmetric).

$$G = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Normalize. Total pairs = 12. So $P(i,j) = G(i,j) / 12$.

$$P = \frac{1}{12}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Compute **Contrast**.

$$\text{Contrast} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} (i-j)^{2}\, P(i,j)$$

Only entries where $i \neq j$ contribute: $P(0,1) = P(1,0) = 2/12 = 1/6$ and $P(2,3) = P(3,2) = 1/6$.

$$\text{Contrast} = 4 \times \left(\frac{1}{6}\right) \times (1)^{2} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 6:** Compute **Homogeneity (Inverse Difference Moment)**.

$$\text{IDM} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \frac{P(i,j)}{1 + (i-j)^{2}}$$

**Step 6a:** Diagonal entries $(i=j)$: $(i-j)^{2}=0$, so the factor is $1/(1+0)=1$.

$$\text{Diagonal contribution} = \frac{1}{12}(2+2+2+2) = \frac{8}{12} = \frac{2}{3}$$

**Step 6b:** Off-diagonal entries $(i-j)^{2}=1$, so the factor is $1/(1+1)=1/2$.

$$\text{Off-diagonal contribution} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 6c:** Sum.

$$\text{IDM} = \frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{4}{6} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$$

**Interpretation:** High homogeneity (0.833) → texture is locally uniform; low contrast (0.667) → small local intensity differences. The ROI resembles a piecewise-constant pattern (two blocks of uniform intensity), consistent with the original image.

---

### 3.4 Compact Derivation of the Compactness Ratio

Starting from the isoperimetric inequality of geometry, for a given area $A$, the **minimum perimeter** is achieved by a circle with $P_{\min} = 2\sqrt{\pi A}$.

A dimensionless **shape factor** can be defined as:

$$C = \frac{P_{\min}^{2}}{P^{2}} = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{2}}$$

This is the same as the compactness formula, confirming that $C \leq 1$ with equality **if and only if** the shape is a perfect circle. For any other shape, $C < 1$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Feature Type | Input Required | Spatial Awareness | Dimensionality | Best Diagnostic Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Binary mask | No (purely geometric) | Low (5–10 features) | Boundary irregularity |
| **Intensity** | Grayscale ROI | No (histogram) | Low (5–10 features) | Density, contrast uptake |
| **Texture** | GLCM (or LBP) | **Yes** (neighbor-based) | Medium (10–20 features) | Tissue heterogeneity, grading |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `shapeFeat`, `texFeat`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
- Node labels use raw uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold, italics, or HTML formatting.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: The Feature Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[RAW BIOMEDICAL IMAGE] --> node2[PREPROCESSING]
    node2 --> node3[IMAGE SEGMENTATION]
    node3 --> node4[ROI EXTRACTION]
    node4 --> node5A[SHAPE FEATURE MODULE]
    node4 --> node5B[INTENSITY FEATURE MODULE]
    node4 --> node5C[TEXTURE FEATURE MODULE]
    node5A --> node6[FEATURE VECTOR FUSION]
    node5B --> node6
    node5C --> node6
    node6 --> node7[CLASSIFIER OR DECISION MODULE]
    node7 --> node8[DIAGNOSTIC OUTPUT]
```

### 4.3 Sub-System Architecture: Shape Feature Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    shape1[Binary Mask Input] --> shape2[Area Computation]
    shape1 --> shape3[Perimeter Computation]
    shape2 --> shape4[Compactness Ratio]
    shape3 --> shape4
    shape1 --> shape5[Moment Calculation]
    shape5 --> shape6[Eccentricity]
    shape5 --> shape7[Solidity]
    shape4 --> shape8[Shape Feature Vector]
    shape6 --> shape8
    shape7 --> shape8
```

### 4.4 Sub-System Architecture: Texture Feature Module (GLCM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    tex1[Grayscale ROI] --> tex2[Define Spatial Offset Delta]
    tex2 --> tex3[Count Co Occurrence Pairs]
    tex3 --> tex4[Build Raw GLCM Matrix]
    tex4 --> tex5[Normalize GLCM to Probabilities]
    tex5 --> tex6A[Compute Contrast]
    tex5 --> tex6B[Compute Homogeneity]
    tex5 --> tex6C[Compute Correlation]
    tex5 --> tex6D[Compute Energy]
    tex5 --> tex6E[Compute Entropy]
    tex6A --> tex7[Haralick Texture Feature Vector]
    tex6B --> tex7
    tex6C --> tex7
    tex6D --> tex7
    tex6E --> tex7
```

### 4.5 Feature Fusion Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuse1[Shape Features FS] --> fuseX[Hybrid Feature Vector F]
    fuse2[Intensity Features FI] --> fuseX
    fuse3[Texture Features FT] --> fuseX
    fuseX --> fuseY[Classifier Input]
```

### 4.6 GLCM Co-occurrence Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    g1[Pixel Value i at x y] --> g2[Pixel Value j at x plus delta y]
    g2 --> g3[Increment GLCM Entry i j]
    g3 --> g4[Final GLCM Matrix]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

---

**Q1. [3 Marks] Define feature extraction in biomedical imaging. List and briefly explain the three principal categories of features.**

> **Model Answer:**
> *Feature extraction is the process of deriving a compact, discriminative numerical representation (feature vector) from raw biomedical image data to support diagnosis, classification, and quantitative analysis.*
>
> The three principal categories are:
> 1. **Shape features** – describe the geometric and morphological properties of a segmented ROI (e.g., area, perimeter, compactness, eccentricity).
> 2. **Texture features** – describe the spatial arrangement and variation of pixel intensities (e.g., Haralick GLCM features, LBP).
> 3. **Intensity features** – describe the statistical distribution of pixel gray-level values (e.g., mean, variance, skewness, entropy).

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] What is the Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)? State the formulas for Contrast and Homogeneity derived from a normalized GLCM.**

> **Model Answer:**
> *The GLCM is a square matrix $G_{\Delta}(i, j)$ that tabulates the frequency with which a pixel of intensity $i$ is spatially adjacent (at a defined offset $\Delta$) to a pixel of intensity $j$. It captures second-order statistical texture information.*
>
> **Contrast:**
> $$\text{Contrast} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} (i-j)^{2}\, P(i,j)$$
>
> **Homogeneity (Inverse Difference Moment):**
> $$\text{Homogeneity} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \frac{P(i,j)}{1 + (i-j)^{2}}$$

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 2024 Scheme Style]**
> *Internal Choice Pattern:* Question A **OR** Question B (each 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks).

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat mathematical expressions, explain the following **shape features** used in biomedical image analysis:
**(i)** Area and Perimeter
**(ii)** Compactness (Circularity)
**(iii)** Eccentricity

**(b) [7 Marks]** A segmented cell nucleus in a histopathology image has been measured. The fitted ellipse has a **major axis length of 40 pixels** and a **minor axis length of 20 pixels**. The **area of the nucleus is 600 pixels** and the **perimeter is 110 pixels**. Compute and interpret the **Compactness** and **Eccentricity** of the nucleus.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 Marks]**

> **Area (A):** Total number of pixels inside the ROI boundary.
> $$A = \sum_{(i,j) \in ROI} 1$$
> *For a binary segmented image, $A$ equals the count of foreground (white) pixels.*

> **Perimeter (P):** Total number of boundary pixels of the ROI (pixels with at least one background neighbor), or the sum of inter-pixel edge lengths.

> **Compactness (C):** A dimensionless ratio from the isoperimetric inequality. $C=1$ for a perfect circle; $C<1$ for irregular shapes.
> $$C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{2}}$$

> **Eccentricity (E):** Ratio of the fitted ellipse's major axis to its minor axis. $E=1$ for a circle; $E \to \infty$ for a line.
> $$E = \frac{\text{major axis length}}{\text{minor axis length}}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Compute Compactness.**

$$C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{2}} = \frac{4 \times \pi \times 600}{110^{2}} = \frac{2400\pi}{12100}$$

$$C = 0.1983 \times 3.1416 \approx 0.6232$$

**Step 2: Compute Eccentricity.**

$$E = \frac{40}{20} = 2.0$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

- The **compactness (0.62)** is well below 1, indicating the nucleus boundary is **irregular** rather than smooth/circular. In histopathology, this is a hallmark of **nuclear pleomorphism** — a key feature in cancer grading.
- The **eccentricity of 2.0** indicates the nucleus is **elongated (ellipsoidal)** — twice as long as it is wide. Elongated nuclei are often associated with malignant transformations in cytology.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **first-order intensity features** (Mean, Variance, Skewness) from the gray-level histogram of a biomedical ROI. State the formula for normalized histogram probability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-bit grayscale ROI (gray levels 0 to 7) has the following histogram: $n(0)=4,\ n(1)=6,\ n(2)=8,\ n(3)=4,\ n(4)=2,\ n(5)=1,\ n(6)=0,\ n(7)=1$. Compute the **Mean**, **Variance**, and **Skewness** of the ROI. Comment on the shape of the histogram.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 Marks]**

> The **normalized histogram** (probability mass function of gray levels) is defined as:
> $$P(g) = \frac{n(g)}{N_p}, \quad g \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, L-1\}$$
> where $n(g)$ is the number of pixels with gray level $g$ and $N_p = \sum_g n(g)$ is the total number of pixels in the ROI.

> **Mean (μ):** Expected gray level.
> $$\mu = \sum_{g=0}^{L-1} g \cdot P(g)$$

> **Variance (σ²):** Second central moment — measures spread.
> $$\sigma^{2} = \sum_{g=0}^{L-1} (g - \mu)^{2}\, P(g)$$

> **Skewness (γ₁):** Third standardized moment — measures histogram asymmetry.
> $$\gamma_{1} = \frac{1}{\sigma^{3}} \sum_{g=0}^{L-1} (g - \mu)^{3}\, P(g)$$

> **Sign convention:** $\gamma_1 > 0$ → histogram tail extends to the right (bright skew); $\gamma_1 < 0$ → tail to the left (dark skew); $\gamma_1 = 0$ → symmetric.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Total pixels $N_p$.**

$$N_p = 4 + 6 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 26$$

**Step 2: Normalized probabilities $P(g)$.**

| $g$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(g)$ | 0.1538 | 0.2308 | 0.3077 | 0.1538 | 0.0769 | 0.0385 | 0.0000 | 0.0385 |

**Step 3: Compute Mean (μ).**

$$\mu = (0)(0.1538) + (1)(0.2308) + (2)(0.3077) + (3)(0.1538) + (4)(0.0769) + (5)(0.0385) + (6)(0) + (7)(0.0385)$$

$$\mu = 0 + 0.2308 + 0.6154 + 0.4614 + 0.3076 + 0.1925 + 0 + 0.2695 = 2.0772$$

**Step 4: Compute Variance (σ²).**

**Step 4a:** Compute $(g - \mu)^{2} \cdot P(g)$ for each $g$.

$$\begin{aligned} g=0:&\ (0 - 2.0772)^{2} \times 0.1538 = (4.3148)(0.1538) = 0.6636 \\ g=1:&\ (1 - 2.0772)^{2} \times 0.2308 = (1.1603)(0.2308) = 0.2678 \\ g=2:&\ (2 - 2.0772)^{2} \times 0.3077 = (0.00596)(0.3077) = 0.00183 \\ g=3:&\ (3 - 2.0772)^{2} \times 0.1538 = (0.8516)(0.1538) = 0.1310 \\ g=4:&\ (4 - 2.0772)^{2} \times 0.0769 = (3.6973)(0.0769) = 0.2843 \\ g=5:&\ (5 - 2.0772)^{2} \times 0.0385 = (8.5429)(0.0385) = 0.3289 \\ g=6:&\ (6 - 2.0772)^{2} \times 0.0000 = 0 \\ g=7:&\ (7 - 2.0772)^{2} \times 0.0385 = (24.2805)(0.0385) = 0.9348 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4b:** Sum the terms.

$$\sigma^{2} = 0.6636 + 0.2678 + 0.0018 + 0.1310 + 0.2843 + 0.3289 + 0 + 0.9348 = 2.6122$$

**Step 4c:** Standard deviation.

$$\sigma = \sqrt{2.6122} \approx 1.6162$$

**Step 5: Compute Skewness (γ₁).**

**Step 5a:** Compute $(g - \mu)^{3} \cdot P(g)$ for each $g$.

$$\begin{aligned} g=0:&\ (-2.0772)^{3} \times 0.1538 = (-8.9632)(0.1538) = -1.3786 \\ g=1:&\ (-1.0772)^{3} \times 0.2308 = (-1.2500)(0.2308) = -0.2885 \\ g=2:&\ (-0.0772)^{3} \times 0.3077 = (-0.00046)(0.3077) = -0.00014 \\ g=3:&\ (0.9228)^{3} \times 0.1538 = (0.7861)(0.1538) = 0.1209 \\ g=4:&\ (1.9228)^{3} \times 0.0769 = (7.1080)(0.0769) = 0.5466 \\ g=5:&\ (2.9228)^{3} \times 0.0385 = (24.9658)(0.0385) = 0.9612 \\ g=6:&\ (3.9228)^{3} \times 0.0000 = 0 \\ g=7:&\ (4.9228)^{3} \times 0.0385 = (119.323)(0.0385) = 4.5939 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5b:** Sum the terms.

$$S_3 = -1.3786 - 0.2885 - 0.00014 + 0.1209 + 0.5466 + 0.9612 + 0 + 4.5939 = 4.5553$$

**Step 5c:** Divide by $\sigma^{3}$.

$$\gamma_{1} = \frac{4.5553}{(1.6162)^{3}} = \frac{4.5553}{4.2212} \approx 1.0792$$

**Step 6: Interpretation of Histogram Shape.**

- The histogram peaks at $g=2$ (probability 0.3077) and has a long tail toward brighter values ($g=4, 5, 7$).
- $\mu \approx 2.08$, $\sigma^{2} \approx 2.61$, and $\gamma_{1} \approx 1.08 > 0$ indicate a **right-skewed (positively skewed) histogram** — the majority of pixels are dark, with a small number of bright outliers. In MRI/CT, this could correspond to a **hypointense lesion with focal hyperintense regions** (e.g., necrotic core with enhancing rim).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation (verified against KTU 2024 marking scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize the GLCM** before computing Haralick features — Examiner deducts **2 marks** if $P(i,j)$ is computed from raw counts instead of normalized probabilities.
> 2. **Inverting the compactness formula** as $P^2 / (4\pi A)$ — This is the reciprocal and yields values $>1$; it is geometrically valid but **not** the convention used in KTU key answers. Always use $C = 4\pi A / P^2$.
> 3. **Using natural log vs. log base 2 for entropy** — KTU accepts either, but you **must state the base** in your answer. Failure to state base = **0.5 mark deduction**.
> 4. **Skipping units/dimensions** in formula derivations — Always state that area is in **pixels²**, perimeter in **pixels**, compactness is **dimensionless**.
> 5. **Confusing GLCM offset directions** — A common mistake is using $(1,1)$ when the question asks for $(0,1)$. Always **explicitly state the offset $\Delta$** you are using.
> 6. **Forgetting to verify $\sum_{i,j} P(i,j) = 1$** for the normalized GLCM — Examiner allocates **1 mark** for this verification step.
> 7. **Skewness sign errors** — A positive skewness **does not** mean "positive values" — it means the **tail** is on the right (positive) side. Misinterpretation = **1 mark deduction**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 — Feature Extraction)**

- **Feature Extraction** transforms raw image pixels into a **feature vector** for downstream CAD tasks.
- **Shape features** (computed on a binary ROI mask): **Area, Perimeter, Compactness, Eccentricity, Solidity, Moments**.
- **Compactness formula:** $C = 4\pi A / P^{2}$, with $C=1$ for a perfect circle and $C<1$ for irregular shapes.
- **Intensity features** (computed from histogram): **Mean, Variance, Skewness, Kurtosis, Energy, Entropy** — all **first-order** (no spatial info).
- **Mean:** $\mu = \sum g \cdot P(g)$; **Variance:** $\sigma^{2} = \sum (g - \mu)^{2} P(g)$; **Entropy:** $H = -\sum P(g) \log_2 P(g)$.
- **Texture features** (second-order, **spatial**): derived from the **GLCM** (Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix).
- **GLCM offset** $\Delta$ must be specified (e.g., $(0,1)$ = horizontal, $(1,0)$ = vertical, $(1,1)$ = diagonal).
- **GLCM must be normalized**: $P(i,j) = G(i,j) / \sum_{i,j} G(i,j)$ and $\sum_{i,j} P(i,j) = 1$.
- **Key Haralick features:** **Contrast** (intensity difference), **Homogeneity/IDM** (local uniformity), **Energy** (orderliness), **Correlation** (linear dependence), **Entropy** (randomness).
- **LBP (Local Binary Pattern):** Encodes local texture by thresholding neighbors with the center pixel — a fast alternative to GLCM.
- **Hybrid feature vector:** $\mathbf{F} = [F_{\text{shape}}; F_{\text{intensity}}; F_{\text{texture}}]$ — fed to a classifier.
- **Shape features are dimensionless ratios**; **intensity features are in gray-level units**; **texture features are dimensionless** (ratios/probabilities).
- **Clinical Applications:** Mammography (mass classification), Histopathology (cancer grading), MRI brain tumor grading, retinal vessel analysis, cell morphology.
- **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Always **state the base of the logarithm** for entropy. Always **normalize the GLCM**. Always **state the offset $\Delta$**.

> **End of Module 3 — Feature Extraction Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEAET636**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feature Selection and Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET636 - BIOMEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feature Selection and Dimensionality Reduction Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Features in Biomedical Imaging?

In biomedical image analysis, a **feature** is a measurable, descriptive attribute extracted from a medical image (CT, MRI, X-ray, PET, ultrasound, histopathology slide, etc.) that quantitatively characterizes tissue, pathology, or anatomical structure. Features can be:

- **Intensity-based**: mean gray-level value, standard deviation, entropy, skewness
- **Texture-based**: GLCM (Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix) features — contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity
- **Shape-based**: area, perimeter, compactness, roundness, fractal dimension
- **Statistical**: first-order histogram moments, higher-order spectral features
- **Transform-domain**: wavelet coefficients, Fourier descriptors, Gabor filter responses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical radiomic or deep-feature pipeline on a 3D MRI volume can yield **thousands to hundreds of thousands of features per patient**, far exceeding the number of available samples ($N$).

This mismatch between feature count ($p$) and sample count ($N$) — i.e., $p \gg N$ — is known as the **Curse of Dimensionality**, and is the central problem this module addresses.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Feature Selection** is the process of identifying and retaining a **subset of the original features** that contribute the most to the predictive task, *without transforming the feature space*. The semantic meaning of the original features is preserved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensionality Reduction** is the process of **projecting or transforming** the original high-dimensional feature space into a lower-dimensional space (a new set of derived variables), generally at the cost of losing the direct interpretability of the original features.

| Aspect | Feature Selection | Dimensionality Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| Output space | Subset of original features | New constructed features (linear or non-linear combinations) |
| Interpretability | High (original units preserved) | Low to Moderate (latent variables) |
| Methods | Filter, Wrapper, Embedded | PCA, LDA, ICA, t-SNE, UMAP, Autoencoders |
| Computational cost | Usually lower | Higher for non-linear methods |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The MRI Toolbox

Imagine you are a radiologist with a toolbox containing **200 diagnostic probes** (features) to evaluate a brain MRI for tumor classification. Many probes are redundant (e.g., mean intensity and median intensity of the same region measure nearly the same thing), and some are noise-dominated (e.g., probe readings from the image corners far from the tumor).

- **Feature Selection** is like **physically removing redundant and noisy tools from the box**, leaving only the 20 most informative probes. The remaining probes are still the *same tools* you started with — you just have fewer of them.
- **Dimensionality Reduction** is like **melting all 200 tools into 5 new "super-tools"**, where each super-tool is a clever weighted combination of the original probes. The super-tools may classify tumors brilliantly, but you can no longer point to any single original tool and say "this one is critical."

> [!TIP]
> In clinical/regulatory contexts (e.g., FDA-cleared Computer-Aided Diagnosis systems), **feature selection is strongly preferred** over dimensionality reduction, because clinicians and regulators require traceability — they want to know *which original feature* drove the diagnosis.

---

## 1.4 The Two-Axes Taxonomy

Feature selection methods lie along two fundamental axes:

1. **Search strategy**: how the candidate subset is generated (exhaustive, greedy, randomized)
2. **Evaluation criterion**: how a candidate subset is scored (statistical dependence, classifier performance, model-internal importance)

Dimensionality reduction methods lie along a different two axes:

1. **Linearity**: linear projection (PCA, LDA) vs. non-linear manifold learning (t-SNE, UMAP, Isomap)
2. **Supervision**: unsupervised (PCA, ICA) vs. supervised (LDA)

These taxonomies together form the conceptual map of this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feature Selection — Three Paradigms

### 2.1.1 Filter Methods (model-independent, fast)

Filter methods rank features by intrinsic statistical properties **before** any classifier is trained.

- **Pearson Correlation Coefficient** — measures linear relationship between feature and class label

$$r_{XY} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

- **ANOVA F-statistic** — for one feature across $K$ classes

$$F = \frac{\text{Between-group variance}}{\text{Within-group variance}} = \frac{\frac{1}{K-1}\sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k(\bar{x}_k - \bar{x})^2}{\frac{1}{N-K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{i \in C_k}(x_i - \bar{x}_k)^2}$$

- **Mutual Information (MI)** — captures **non-linear** dependence

$$I(X;Y) = \sum_{x \in X}\sum_{y \in Y} p(x,y) \log\frac{p(x,y)}{p(x)\,p(y)}$$

- **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test** — for categorical features/labels

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c}\frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why filter methods matter in biomedical imaging**: They are **clinically interpretable** (a feature with a high F-statistic is a real discriminative biomarker) and computationally cheap, but they ignore feature-feature redundancy.

### 2.1.2 Wrapper Methods (classifier-dependent, accurate)

Wrapper methods use a classifier's performance as the evaluation criterion. The search can be:

- **Forward Selection** — start empty, add the feature that most improves performance
- **Backward Elimination** — start with all features, remove the one whose removal hurts the least
- **Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)** — train, rank by coefficient magnitude, remove weakest, repeat
- **Sequential Floating Search (SFFS/SFBS)** — allows both adds and removes dynamically

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an imaging dataset with $p = 1000$ features, exhaustive search requires $2^{1000}$ evaluations — **computationally infeasible**. Wrappers therefore rely on greedy or randomized search, which can get stuck in **local optima**.

### 2.1.3 Embedded Methods (selection is part of training)

The classifier itself performs feature selection during training.

- **LASSO** ($\ell_1$-regularized regression): drives coefficients of irrelevant features **exactly to zero**
- **Ridge** ($\ell_2$-regularized regression): shrinks coefficients but never to zero
- **Elastic Net**: combined $\ell_1 + \ell_2$ penalty — handles correlated features
- **Tree-based importance**: Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost feature importance scores

> [!TIP]
> In modern radiomics pipelines, **LASSO with cross-validation** is the de-facto standard for selecting a sparse set of stable radiomic biomarkers.

---

## 2.2 Dimensionality Reduction — The Major Methods

### 2.2.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — Unsupervised, Linear

PCA finds orthogonal axes (principal components) that **maximize the variance** of the projected data.

Given a centered data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$ (rows = samples, columns = features), PCA solves:

$$\max_{w : \lVert w \rVert = 1} \text{Var}(Xw) = w^{\top}\Sigma w$$

where $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N-1}X^{\top}X$ is the covariance matrix. The solution is given by the **eigen-decomposition**:

$$\Sigma w_j = \lambda_j w_j$$

The $j$-th principal component score is $z_j = X w_j$, with variance $\lambda_j$.

### 2.2.2 Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) — Supervised, Linear

LDA finds axes that **maximize between-class separation** while **minimizing within-class scatter**.

$$J(w) = \frac{w^{\top} S_B \, w}{w^{\top} S_W \, w}$$

where

$$S_B = \sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k (\mu_k - \mu)(\mu_k - \mu)^{\top}$$

$$S_W = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k)(x_i - \mu_k)^{\top}$$

LDA produces **at most $K-1$ discriminant components** for $K$ classes.

### 2.2.3 Independent Component Analysis (ICA) — Unsupervised, Non-Gaussian

ICA seeks components that are **statistically independent** (stronger than uncorrelated). For whitened data $z$, it finds an unmixing matrix $W$ such that the components $s = Wz$ have **maximally non-Gaussian** distributions, typically measured by **neg-entropy**:

$$J(s) = H(z_{\text{gauss}}) - H(s) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}[\log\cosh(s)]$$

ICA is widely used in **EEG/MEG signal decomposition** (removing ocular and cardiac artifacts).

### 2.2.4 t-SNE — Non-linear, Visualization

t-SNE models pairwise similarities as conditional probabilities in both high and low dimensions, and **minimizes the Kullback-Leibler divergence** between the two distributions:

$$p_{j \vert i} = \frac{\exp(-\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}{\sum_{k \neq i}\exp(-\lVert x_i - x_k \rVert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}, \quad q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}(1 + \lVert y_k - y_l \rVert^2)^{-1}}$$

$$\text{Cost} = D_{KL}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> t-SNE is for **visualization only** (2D/3D), not for classification. Distances and densities between clusters in t-SNE plots are **not meaningful** — only local cluster structure is preserved.

### 2.2.5 UMAP — Non-linear, Fast, Preserves Global Structure

UMAP is based on **Riemannian geometry** and **algebraic topology**, optimized via cross-entropy minimization. It is **faster than t-SNE**, scales better, and preserves more global structure, making it the modern standard for biomedical single-cell and imaging datasets.

### 2.2.6 Autoencoders — Non-linear, Learned

A neural network trained to **reconstruct its input** through a bottleneck of size $k \ll p$. The bottleneck activations form the reduced representation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method | Objective | Type | Output Dim Limit | Key Equation / Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pearson Correlation | Linear dep. on label | Filter | $1$ per feature | $r_{XY}$ above |
| ANOVA F-test | Group mean separation | Filter | $1$ per feature | $F$ above |
| Mutual Information | Non-linear dep. | Filter | $1$ per feature | $I(X;Y)$ above |
| $\chi^2$ Test | Categorical dep. | Filter | $1$ per feature | $\chi^2$ above |
| LASSO | Sparsity in regression | Embedded | Up to $p$ non-zero | $\min \lVert y - X\beta \rVert_2^2 + \lambda \lVert \beta \rVert_1$ |
| RFE | Recursive elimination | Wrapper | Search-controlled | $\text{rank} = \text{sort}(\lvert \beta \rvert)$ |
| PCA | Maximize variance | Linear, unsup. | $p$ | $\Sigma w = \lambda w$ |
| LDA | Maximize $S_B / S_W$ | Linear, sup. | $K-1$ | Rayleigh quotient $J(w)$ above |
| ICA | Maximize independence | Non-Gaussian | $p$ | Neg-entropy $J(s)$ above |
| t-SNE | Preserve neighbors | Non-linear | $2$ or $3$ | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q)$ above |
| UMAP | Preserve topology | Non-linear | Any | Cross-entropy on fuzzy sets |
| Autoencoder | Reconstruct input | Non-linear | Bottleneck size | $\min \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **explained variance ratio** in PCA is computed as $\text{EVR}_j = \frac{\lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i}$. To retain $\geq 95\%$ of total variance, choose the smallest $k$ such that $\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j \geq 0.95 \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i$.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Biomedical Imaging

| Application | Typical Pipeline |
|---|---|
| **Radiomics for tumor grading** | Extract $\sim 1000$ features $\rightarrow$ LASSO selection $\rightarrow$ SVM classifier |
| **CAD for mammography** | Texture features $\rightarrow$ ANOVA filter $\rightarrow$ LDA projection $\rightarrow$ Logistic regression |
| **EEG/MEG artifact removal** | ICA decomposition $\rightarrow$ manual/semi-automatic component rejection |
| **Single-cell imaging cytometry** | t-SNE / UMAP for 2D visualization of cell populations |
| **MRI segmentation (deep learning)** | Encoder-decoder CNN learns implicit dimensionality reduction in latent space |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of PCA (Geometric & Variational)

Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$ be the data matrix (each row is one patient/sample, each column is one feature), and assume columns have been **mean-centered** so that $\sum_i x_i = 0$.

**Step 1 — Formulate the variance of a 1-D projection**

For a unit vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^p$ ($\lVert w \rVert = 1$), the projection of the centered data onto $w$ gives scalar scores $z = Xw$. The variance of $z$ is:

$$\text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i^{\top} w)^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} w^{\top} X^{\top} X w$$

**Step 2 — Identify the matrix**

The sample covariance matrix is:

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{N-1} X^{\top} X \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$$

So the objective becomes:

$$\max_{w : \lVert w \rVert = 1} \; w^{\top} \Sigma w$$

**Step 3 — Set up the Lagrangian**

To incorporate the unit-norm constraint, introduce a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^{\top} \Sigma w - \lambda(w^{\top} w - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $w$ and set to zero**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2 \Sigma w - 2 \lambda w = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Sigma w = \lambda w$$

This is the **eigenvalue equation**. Thus $w$ must be an eigenvector of $\Sigma$, and the optimal $w$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the **largest** eigenvalue (so that the maximized variance equals $\lambda_{\max}$).

**Step 5 — Generalize to $k$ components**

Subsequent principal components $w_2, w_3, \ldots, w_k$ are the eigenvectors corresponding to $\lambda_2 \geq \lambda_3 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_k$, subject to mutual orthogonality $w_i^{\top} w_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$. Together, $\{w_1, \ldots, w_k\}$ form the **loading matrix** $W \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times k}$.

**Step 6 — Compute the reduced representation**

$$Z = X W \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times k}$$

Each row of $Z$ is the $k$-dimensional coordinate of the corresponding patient in the principal-component space.

**Step 7 — Variance retained**

$$\text{Total variance retained} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Toy Radiomics Dataset)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario**: 4 patients × 2 radiomic features (Feature A = tumor intensity, Feature B = tumor texture energy), 2-class problem (Benign vs Malignant).

| Patient | Feature A | Feature B | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 2 | 3 | Benign |
| $P_2$ | 3 | 4 | Benign |
| $P_3$ | 6 | 8 | Malignant |
| $P_4$ | 7 | 9 | Malignant |

**Step 1 — Center the data**

$$\bar{A} = \frac{2+3+6+7}{4} = 4.5, \quad \bar{B} = \frac{3+4+8+9}{4} = 6$$

Centered matrix:

$$X_c = \begin{bmatrix} -2.5 & -3 \\ -1.5 & -2 \\ 1.5 & 2 \\ 2.5 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the covariance matrix**

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{N-1} X_c^{\top} X_c = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix}(-2.5)^2 + (-1.5)^2 + 1.5^2 + 2.5^2 & (-2.5)(-3) + (-1.5)(-2) + 1.5(2) + 2.5(3) \\ \text{symmetric} & (-3)^2 + (-2)^2 + 2^2 + 3^2\end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 6.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 & 7.5 + 3 + 3 + 7.5 \\ 7.5 + 3 + 3 + 7.5 & 9 + 4 + 4 + 9\end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 17.0 & 21.0 \\ 21.0 & 26.0\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 5.667 & 7.000 \\ 7.000 & 8.667\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvalue equation**

$$\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = (5.667 - \lambda)(8.667 - \lambda) - 49 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - 14.334\lambda + (49.111 - 49) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lambda^2 - 14.334\lambda + 0.111 = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{14.334 \pm \sqrt{14.334^2 - 4(0.111)}}{2} = \frac{14.334 \pm \sqrt{205.473 - 0.444}}{2} = \frac{14.334 \pm 14.321}{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 \approx 14.328, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 0.006$$

**Step 4 — Variance retained**

$$\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{14.328}{14.328 + 0.006} \approx 0.9996 = 99.96\%$$

A single principal component captures **99.96%** of the variance — the 2-D problem effectively collapses to 1-D.

**Step 5 — First principal component direction**

For $\lambda_1$:

$$(\Sigma - 14.328 I)w_1 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \begin{bmatrix} -8.661 & 7.000 \\ 7.000 & -5.661\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} w_{11} \\ w_{12}\end{bmatrix} = 0$$

$$-8.661\, w_{11} + 7.000\, w_{12} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; w_{11} = 0.8083\, w_{12}$$

Normalizing: $w_{11}^2 + w_{12}^2 = 1 \Rightarrow w_{12} = 0.589, \, w_{11} = 0.808$

$$w_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.589\end{bmatrix}$$

This is the direction in feature space that maximally separates the two classes — exactly what a radiologist would want for diagnosis.

---

## 3.3 LDA Derivation (Sketch)

**Step 1 — Define the two scatter matrices**

$$S_B = N_1(\mu_1 - \mu)(\mu_1 - \mu)^{\top} + N_2(\mu_2 - \mu)(\mu_2 - \mu)^{\top}$$

$$S_W = \sum_{i \in C_1} (x_i - \mu_1)(x_i - \mu_1)^{\top} + \sum_{i \in C_2} (x_i - \mu_2)(x_i - \mu_2)^{\top}$$

**Step 2 — Fisher's criterion**

$$J(w) = \frac{w^{\top} S_B w}{w^{\top} S_W w}$$

**Step 3 — Maximize (differentiate, set to zero)**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; S_B w = J(w) \, S_W w$$

Assuming $S_W$ is invertible, this becomes a **generalized eigenvalue problem**:

$$S_W^{-1} S_B w = \lambda w$$

The optimal discriminant direction is the eigenvector corresponding to the **largest** eigenvalue of $S_W^{-1} S_B$. For $K=2$ classes, only **one** non-trivial eigenvalue exists, giving a 1-D projection.

---

## 3.4 LASSO Objective (Full Form)

For a regression/classification problem with feature matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$ and label vector $y \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$, the LASSO estimate is:
